Hello everyone!I am thrilled to be taking this course, as global gender issues are my passion and the area in which I plan to build a career. I am an American currently serving as a Peace Corps Volunteer in a rural area of southern Morocco. I primarily work at a youth center, teaching English and leading other enrichment activities, but I have also spent a large amount of my time working specifically with women and girls in my community on various projects they've identified as important.After studying Women and Gender Studies in the midwestern US, coming to Morocco has added an entirely new lens to my perspective about global women's rights, particularly as it relates to culture and tradition. Cultural beliefs are often an important factor in the persistence of problematic gender roles and the denial of basic human rights to women. This can pose an interesting problem when met with proponents of cultural relativism, who might say that preserving the supposed \purity\" of a culture or its traditions is the most important thing, even if that means denying access of human rights to women or other groups. Much has been written on this subject by scholars and activists alike, but the point that resonates the most with me, particularly in light of what I've learned during my time in Morocco, is that cultures are not static - they are dynamic, always changing. Many cultural relativists are advocating protecting a very singular, unchanging view of a given culture, when in reality cultures are continually changing and adapting over the course of time. Furthermore, there are often multiple different traditions and interpretations of those traditions within any given culture. Within Morocco, for example, there are Arab communities, Amazigh (Berber) communities, Saharawi communities, and others, all with differing views and traditions, including those about gender and the family. Those who make arguments against changes to the Moroccan family code on the basis of \"preserving culture\" are making the mistake of generalizing a very diverse culture into a monolithic, unchanging entity, which is false and only serves to further marginalize those from minority communities.I am very excited to continue this dialogue with other members of the course as we continue to learn about the complexities of global women's heath and human rights."

First and foremost, as I was reading the article I have to say that I do not agree with, \Disparities in wealth are less visible in Americans’ everyday lives today than they were a century ago. Even poor people have televisions, air conditioners and cars.\" It is true that even people of lesser income may have cars this day in age, but the hyper rich have \"super cars\" or Lamborghinis, Ferraris, Maseratis, etc. that are quite \"visible\" and show off an immense amount of wealth. This day in age, people have Rolexes that are worth more than what an average American makes in a whole year. I think the point trying to be made here was that the average quality of life has increased in the United States and I would agree with that point, but I think the author definitely went the wrong way about describing this. The article uses the Gini coefficient in his or her analysis and speculates that rich and developed countries have a lower coefficient than those of developing countries where there is large scale disparity. The author uses the coefficient to argue that although the coefficient has increased in some countries, on a larger scale it has decreased. The article cites the Kuznets curve which says that inequality rises in the early stages of industrialization as people leave the land and become more productive and earn more in factories, and then as more people make the jump industrialization starts to become completed and better educated citizens demand for the wealth to be redistributed. I agree with the points the author is making because they all seem sensible and intuitive. The article goes on to argue that although discrepancy in education is a consideration for the gap in income, the failure of government policy has also been a big factor such as in developing countries the large income gap is actually counter productive to growth as an entire country. I agree that education should be prioritized and the government should take steps to prevent social immobility. I think they follow the economic explanation provided throughout the entire article and in order to advance even further as a country, gaps in income need to be shrunk."

I always like to emphasize with my students that mistakes are how we learn. At the start of the school year, we discuss the fact that if none of us made mistakes, then we really wouldn't need to be in school because we'd all be perfect! Therefore, mistakes are encouraged and analyzed together so we can learn. I take the pressure off individual students by shifting the paradigm. Instead of, \What mistakes did some of you make on this problem?\" I ask, \"What are some common errors students might make on a problem like this one?\" This allows students to discuss frequent errors without feeling any sense of ownership or embarrassment from a mistake. Once we have developed the culture, most students openly own their mistakes and gladly share so others can learn from them."

In Senegal Italian development cooperation has financed a project on girls' education focusing on primary school. It provided scholarship for girls, psychological and pedagogic assistance, mentoring, training for teachers and curricula revision as well as all the other key points in order to promote girls education such as communication and awareness campaigns with communities, parents, religious leaders, teachers and so on. Moreover, in order to make activities sustainable, the project promoted economic activities for the mothers’ organisations, working within the schools, providing services for students at low cost (uniforms, meals, etc.).This approach has been recognised by the Senegalese Ministry of education and cited in the new sectorial programme for education as a best practice in order to enhance its gender policy. As a consequence, Italian cooperation has decided to finance another phase of the same programme. This phase will put the focus on secondary education and all the aspects relating to reproductive health as well as women rights as human rights. The programme will also give an important place to women alphabetisation, giving a second chance to young women who dropped out.

We do not use ability grouping for math in my school. Two years ago, in 3rd grade (which is the grade I teach) our administrator asked the 3rd grade team to split our students by ability for reading. We did this for several reasons, but primarily because we had to document for RTI (Response to Intervention) for those students reading below grade level. We used several tools to determine where to place children and the children were never told that they were being \grouped\", just that we were changing teachers for reading groups. [And, yes, some of them could figure out what we were doing.] The groups were fluid, however, so that if a child made significant progress in reading ability, he or she was \"moved\" to another teacher (essentially another/higher group). What we discovered was that those children who were so very low that they couldn't read was that once they were placed in a very small class, they began to participate more AND their reading difficulties became more obvious. Why? Because suddenly those children could not disappear into the larger group. They felt more at ease responding because they were in the same boat as everyone else at the reading table. Most of those children made huge strides in their reading levels.As a teacher I noticed that even though we used several tools, some children were incorrectly placed. The students in my class were reading way beyond the 3rd grade level and were considered the advanced group; however, some of those students simply did not have the maturity to be in such a class and found themselves way over their heads.I know this is not about math and I recognize the problems with grouping or labeling; however, I see the benefits for those 3rd graders who are so woefully behind in their reading skills. They need that small group setting--those intervention strategies to bring them up to speed."

Interesting article — thanks.> As far as I know, the p-Value is to be interpreted as> \Assuming that the null hypothesis is true, how likely is it> to see data as extreme or extremer than the data we have> seen.\"That sounds correct.> This sounds rather reliable, so where is the catch?## **Firstly**The p-value doesn't tell how likely it is that that the nullis true in the first place. In particular, a low p-valuedoes **not** necessarily mean that the null is unlikely.## **Secondly**If the p-value is 0.05, it means that there's a 5 percentchance that the results could have happened even if therewere no real effect.This means that 5 percent of tests where there's no realeffect will give \"significant\" p-values just due to thenatural random variability.----**More detail on first point**To make up an outlandish example...Suppose we want to study whether Professor Hastie has beenabducted by aliens.We will study this question by observing whether he postsany comments on the forum today.Our null hypothesis is that he has *not* been abducted byaliens.Assume, for the sake of argument, that Professor Hastieposts comments on 96 percent of days. (This may not beaccurate, but the story is more colorful with a high-profilefigure.)Suppose at the end of the day, there is no post fromProfessor Hastie.Well, we know there's only a 0.04 chance of that happeningwhen the null is true (that is, when he is not abducted byaliens.)So the p-value is 0.04, which is less than 0.05.Does that mean that we've got good evidence that ProfessorHastie has been abducted by aliens ?Of course not.In particular, does it mean that there's only a 4 percentchance the null is true ?No.If you are highly skeptical about alien abduction, you willsay that the \"prior\" probability of alien abduction is solow, that the posterior probability is still low (eventhough the lack of postings is consistent with alien abduction.)----**More detail on second point**What the article seems to be concerned about is the following.If there are a lot of people doing tests where there's noreal effect, 5 percent of these people will get\"significant\" results by chance. Call these \"falsepositives\".If, in addition, there are only a few doing tests wherethere *is* a real effect, the total number of falsepositives could be more than the number of real positives.If all \"significant\" result were published, this could meanthat most published"

I would have my students think of a time they felt like they grew the most academically. I would then have them identify the qualities that led to that growth. Hopefully as a result of this, things like being challenged, making mistakes, trying things new ways would be mentioned. From there I would move into the idea that the brain tends to grow more and we tend to learn more when we make a mistake. Four years ago, my first year teaching, I was warned about a \difficult\" student before the start of the school year. I was told to put his mother's phone number on my cell so I could call her immediately when he gave me trouble. No one had anything positive to say about him. I was troubled by the universally negative view of this child and was determined to give him a positive experience in a classroom. During the first week I called his mom. She picked up the phone and said, \"What has he done now?\" I told her how nice it was to have him in my class (I had made sure to make the call during the \"honeymoon\" period) and I gave specific examples of things he had done successfully in class and mentioned that I saw that he clearly had the potential to do well. I heard a huge sigh of relief. We talked about working together to make this year a good stepping off point for high school and that I wanted to try to channel his energy into being a leader. Throughout the year, I continued to reinforce my belief in his potential by identifying examples of clear mathematical thinking even when he might have made arithmetic errors. His mom and I spoke frequently and only one of those conversations had to be about \"problem\" behavior. This experience was not universal among his other teachers.I guess this is a kind of long winded way of giving an example of the positive impact of demonstrating true belief in a student's potential by looking past the \"bad kid\" stereotype with which he had always lived."

As an in-class middle and high school tutor, I see many students lacking the foundational knowledge of mathematics. Just to clarify, I work with students in their math class ranging from Pre-Algebra to Pre-Calculus in low income areas. During the school year, I witness teachers skimming over the basics with the assumption their students should have already acquired those basic facts since they are in a higher level of math only to see their students fail. Some skipped facts can be as simple as adding and subtracting integers or fractions. The students I have worked with often have these misconceptions and required re-teaching in order to advance. But it's unfair to put the blame on the teacher solely. Students are embarrassed to ask their teachers questions out of fear of their peers. They fear of being mocked and ridiculed. I also have met students who truly believe they are terrible in math and give up the moment they make a mistake. They fear of failing so they avoid doing the work in the first place. It's frustrating to see children with low self-esteem and a closed-mind about themselves. But changing their beliefs cannot be made in a day. Sometimes it takes weeks, sometimes years. Sometimes I can't reach them at all and I don't see them the following school year. It's hard to tell if algebra is difficult if the student doesn't try in the first place.

I teach 8th grade classes that are not grouped. Most students come to my class having gained strong procedural skills from the previous grades. My goal is for students to understand the content at a deep level, and my style is different than what students have experienced in their past courses. Sometimes, a special ed student, perhaps one who typically struggles in math, will ask a question that gets at the heart of the mathematics of a problem. For example, why is the area of a triangle equal to half of the base times the height of the triangle? Furthermore, a verbal explanation may not be good enough, and if the culture of the classroom is safe enough for kids to expose what they don't know, that's when the real, deep learning begins for everyone!The main issue that I'm struggling with right now, is taking the risk of investing enough time to probe deeply in a math concept while the clock is ticking on my 38-minute class, the 180 days are flying by, and the mountain of Common Core State Standards is still in front of us. We were told that the Common Core would contain fewer topics that go deeper into understanding, but at the middle school and high school levels, that just isn't true. So, I feel forced to think and act in a traditional, fixed mindset - if we group the students, we can move faster through the content and meet more of the learning objectives.

I think this falls on the teacher. Someone spoke to this above... because if you have a teacher that is just terrible and doesn't know how to manage a class of mixed ability, you get very bored \gifted\" students. However, if the teacher knows how to differentiate properly, they can challenge your child, within the same confines of the concept being learned, at a deeper level. Not just making your child do MORE work... but have your child do DIFFERENT work around the same topic. Sometimes that can be as easy as asking one question to take it deeper. The problem I've seen with this, however, is that sometimes those \"gifted\" students still know in their head that they are getting different work. They still think that they're smarter than the other kids, so they will tend to call the class boring. Even though the teacher is definitely challenging the kid. I did an simulation of normal data in R and used it to create paired data, which I tested using both parametric (t) and non-parametric (wilcoxon) tests. I varied the effect size and the standard error. What I found was quite interesting. Indeed, the t-test definitely shows a larger p-value for the unpaired vs paired test, except when the SE becomes very large compared to the effect size. The interesting thing is that the Wilcoxon test doesn't follow this rule: there were some (though not many) samples, where the paired test p-value was larger than that of the unpaired test (ie. the unpaired test was more significant then the paired test). So, the question is, why would the nonparametric test (sometimes) show a lower p-value for the unpaired test vs paired test?

JamesL, I'm afraid you are confused. I am not talking about sampling. I am asking whether the distribution of heights actually follows a normal distribution. This has nothing to do with sampling. Sampling from a non-normal distribution many times does not convert the distribution into a normal distribution. It may let you construct statistics which are approximately normal, but it does not change the distribution. If you still want to talk about sampling, please make your own thread. I'm talking about the distribution itself.By the way, you seem to be suggesting that all distributions are normal, although it is hard to tell because you may be using the equals sign to mean something other than equality. The space of distributions has infinitely many dimensions. Normal distributions are a very, very special two-dimensional family of distributions. They have many properties not shared with many other typically encountered distributions. For example, they are not skewed. They are continuous and unimodal. They have subexponential tails. So, it is a very strong assertion to say that a distribution (such as that of heights) is normal.

Australia became a signatory to the CEDAW Treaty in July 1980, with ratification in July 1983 (effective 27 August 1983). A declaration was added regarding the sharing/distribution of constitutional power between the Commonwealth and the states and territories of Australia effecting implementation of the Treaty. There were two reservations added: The first stating that the government was not in a position to introduce paid maternity leave or comparable social benefits throughout Australia. The second reservation advised that the government was unwilling to alter the Defence Force policy excluding women from combat and combat-related duties. (A subsequent review of the definitions of combat and combat-related duties was to be undertaken by the Australian government but the reservation still stands). Despite encouragement for Australia to remove its reservations as soon as possible, a 2010 review and 2012 interim report has seen them unchanged.

I've taught at several schools over the course of my career. Most recently I've been in a school that tracks for math, language arts, and social studies. When I first came to the school I convinced the math department to reduce the number of tracks but ultimately lost that battle and they went up to five tracks per grade in middle school. Perhaps trying to mask the lowest group, it's called 'enrichment' rather than 'remediation'. I had previously taught at a school that had multi-age grouping in which students were grouped by ability for some units (like procedural content) but were mixed 'ability' for others such as geometry. I use 'ability' in the quotes to emphasize that I really think it was still confusing ability and performance. That meant that students were re-evaluated at each unit which did result in some mixing and less clear identification of the low v high group. I understand why the tracking occurs; people think it serves the students because the high achievers can fly without encumbrance and the low achievers can get the attention they need. It is also hard to teach students at many different levels, especially with classes of 30 - 40 students for 46 minute periods. On the other hand, it is just an illusion that students who are ability grouped are all at the same level. It kind of gives the teacher the excuse not to individualize the instruction. There is also tremendous pressure from parents of higher achieving kids to separate their children from the pack. I've felt that this grouping does not help students, and I appreciate the specific research you've cited that I can use as evidence to support my own experience.

Can anyone explain how we know that the firms' profits will be reduced?Presumably we are in a competitive market initially, so profits are zero and production is at the market equilibrium. Now the government introduces the wage cap. This causes a movement along the labour supply curve - the price of labour has been set, so the quantity supplied will drop accordingly. Firms demand more labour than is available, so there is a deficit of labour, but the cap prevents them from raising wages in response. As the quantity of labour is reduced, the quantity of goods produced is reduced. This reduces the marginal cost of production (diminishing returns on labour mean it's more efficient to produce smaller amounts).At this lower quantity of production, demand is higher than supply, so the price is higher than the marginal cost of producing the goods. This leads to a deadweight loss in the market, but also a profit for the firms. So, lower quantity, higher price, lower marginal costs. Unless we know the elasticities of supply and demand, for both labour and the good(s) being produced, we don't know the relative sizes of these effects. So how can we say whether profits will be higher or lower?

I can understand the principle of using an active voice and writing in the first person but to do so on the basis that readers will otherwise be unwilling or unable to tackle the material seems rather condescending. Although I only teach at High School level and from an IT and business perspective, my first imperative to my students from age 11 onwards is to always start any document design from a consideration of audience and purpose. If I am writing for my younger students it is entirely appropriate to use simple structures and to explain specialist vocabulary before using it (the teaching of the correct use of specialist language is one of the core requirements for all subject teachers in England). My older students would be expected both to understand and use more complex and nuanced language forms.When writing for a specialised audience of fellow professionals, are we really to conclude that our readers' interest is so transitory that the lack of 'spunky' active verbs will deter them from reading a paper? Are our peers so naive as to be unable to distinguish that the passive voice implicitly indicates the authors as subjects? When a paper says \The data were analysed...\" readers will surely understand that to mean \"The researchers analysed the data...\" unless it is specified otherwise.When writing for a general audience the language needs to be simplified and any specialist terms explained. Non-specialist readers of a wide circulation publication may have only a passing interest in an article and so it would be entirely appropriate to use an active voice in the first person in order to capture and maintain their attention. Whether the same does, or should, apply to readers of academic publications is, in my view, another matter. I don't feel I need my hand held and sweeties held out to persuade me to read a paper if it is relevant to my interests or needs. I just want the content to be clearly and succinctly expressed and, again, in my opinion, that can be accomplished at least as well in the passive voice as the active.Sorry for a rather long post which probably makes me look like something of a linguistic conservative, not to say dinosaur. I have to admit that I am the only person of my acquaintance to to use the pronoun 'one' in conversation!"

I often talk about learning synapses and such to my first graders, but what they enjoy most is growing the class brain. At the beginning of the year, I get one of those toy 'grow a brain' (from novelty stores). I talk about how small it is at the beginning of the school year. Then I put it in a vase (ideally one which has a small opening, large middle). Each day a student adds a teaspoon or two of water to represent daily learning. The water enlarges the brain. Truthfully, at the beginning, I sneak a little extra water on it to get it going. During breaks, some water evaporates and the brain can get a bit dirty. I tell the student that the brain did not grow over break because no one was in the class feeding it with learning (water). Prediction intervals and confidence intervals apply to different things. A confidence interval is an interval that (hopefully) includes a distribution parameter, which is an unknown, but fixed number. A prediction interval is an interval that is meant to include the value of a random variable, i.e. something that will change every time it's observed.

To answer the opening questions first:1. Marginal cost increases as more is produced because of the diminishing returns of labor - the marginal product of labor decreases as more is produced. Your costs for each subsequent item produced increases at a faster rate the more you produce.2. Profit maximization means that firms will try to maximize revenue while minimizing costs (since profit is revenue minus costs). For a given market price, this means they want to produce as much as possible to sell while keeping cost as low as possible. The marginal cost is the amount that it costs to produce one additional unit of an item. If this cost is more than the firm can sell that item for, it will not be following the profit maximization mantra of maximizing revenue while minimizing costs. Costs will have exceeded potential revenue.3. In a competitive market, firms or producers are not setting the price. As such, they have to produce based on the market price, so the only way for them to maximize profits would be to make sure that marginal costs do not exceed the price (their potential revenue). In a non-competitive market or one where producers could set the price, they could simply charge over the marginal cost for a given production quantity, ensuring that their revenue exceeds their costs. This would allow for theoretically limitless profit.The oil market appears to be not perfectly competitive from the little information that was contained in the article. If we think of the entire country of Libya as an oil \firm\" for simplicity, than the introduction of an additional mere 500,000 barrels of oil a day into the market should not really affect the price so immediately. I think it is difficult to say, however, that the oil market is not at all competitive from just this information. By the textbook definition of a competitive market that we learned in this section, it is not competitive because no single firm should be able to affect the market price. But the increase in supply would create a temporary surplus that would drive down the price of oil as whole, which is a response to competitive market forces (consumer demand and supply available). So from that standpoint, the market does seem competitive.I understand that in a competitive market, producers do not set the price. But the idea that they cannot affect the market price at all seems like an oversimplification. Maybe I am misunderstanding this principle.Regardless, the marginal c"

Instead of putting students into ability groups, a colleague of mine makes a sign-up sheet that lists different groups. She strives for a status-free classroom. She explains that students have different experiences and therefore different needs in different math areas. They may have been absent or not had many experiences in their lives with certain math concepts. It is very much a growth mindset attitude. The groups are based on students' self-assessing their own needs. For example, the fish tag group is for students who would like to have her check in often at first. The lighthouse group is for students who would like to have her check in briefly and less often. The sailing group is for students who feel confident to work on their own. And the extra sail group would like to go faster and need an extra challenge. The groups allow for her to address their needs quickly and effectively. Does anyone have comments they would like to add to this kind of grouping?

I teach in a school for gifted children. Teaching math in a classroom with a wide range of achievement levels reinforced anxiety in children for whom math was a struggle. They hyperfocused on what they couldn't do because they were aware of how some children \got it\" and they didn't. We started grouping the children across third and fourth grade based, not on ability, but based on learning style, experience with concepts, and achievement level. There is still a bell-curve within in group, but all the kids are deemed intellectually gifted. The groupings have truly helped to minimize the performance anxiety that previously existed. The groupings are flexible and kids can move in and out of groups if their experience in a particular strand matches better with another grouping."

I believe that with the exception of, more or less, the top five high school players, everyone should complete four years of college. The basis for my opinion is two-fold: firstly, if the player does not succeed for whatever reason in the NBA, he has his four year degree to fall back on. This will allow him to generate income that he would have foregone had he not completed his studies.Secondly, if the player does succeed in the NBA, the four years spent in college will have permitted him to receive a higher degree of basketball training, enhancing his abilities, and allowing him to have a higher competitive advantage over players who had chosen to leave college early. This competitive advantage translates to a higher source of income, considering the fact that the highest skilled players receive the highest salaries. In both cases the opportunity cost of not finishing college are higher that the alternative, because of lost income in the event of a player failing in the NBA and lost income in the event of success in the NBA.

First, education for all children is an important piece to solving this puzzle. That being said, I understand that is a challenging task. I am reminded of an article I read years ago that profiled an ingenious project for educating street children in South America. Education \cubes\" were crafted out of wood and chalkboards. Inside the cube/box were pencils, chalk, food and other teaching materials. The cube was mounted on 4 wheels and a rope was attached. In a small amount of time, with only a few items a \"mobile classroom\" was created. This classroom could be easily pulled to town squares, alleys, slums, or other hangouts and children were free to gather around and learn. Bringing the school to the children minimized significant barriers that would have prevented them from accessing school altogether. I am unfamiliar with the success of this program, but it separates the notion of schooling from education, focusing more on creative ways to deliver education rather than \"how do we get the kids to our school?\". In countries facing high levels of HIV/AIDS, this idea could be modified to fit the terrain, climate or demographic. This idea of mobile and portable education can also be applied to sexual health education.In Ontario, Canada there were studies done on intimate partner violence, and it was found that neighbours, friends and families of victims and perpetrators often knew much more than police did about IPV. I have always been against set grouping of students. Students change so much as they grow and learn. They are not all on the same trajectory when they learn, and they may be able to advance quickly in some areas but not in others. I teach kindergarten, and I pull students to work with me on various tasks in small groups, but the groups are formed based on a particular skill or learning concept and the group members need. They are not pegged into one group for the whole year. My groups change as often as the skill or concept has been learned. Some students exit the group quickly and sometimes others move in. Many times what many would consider a traditionally high student will be with a traditionally low student because they both need practice with a particular skill or concept. I don't need to slow down a whole group of students because one needs a lesson, nor do I stop a student from learning something new simply because the others in the group are not ready. Being flexible with the students in a classroom is key to helping them get the right lessons at the right time.

Our school has a no tracking policy, but we do allow some 6th graders to skip 6th grade math and move onto Pre-Algebra. This is allowing us to identify a smaller number of students who are truly ready to move ahead in math and continue to be successful.The one area of tracking that we have been trying for 3 years is similar to one mentioned in a couple of other posts: We have a 2 period math class for pre-algebra. This is designed for students who have struggled in math. Our experience has been similar to the other posts in that we see significant improvement in math success defined by reduction in negative math attitudes, a increase in perseverance (growth mind set?) and increase in assessment scores. In the beginning, the students do seem to feel very \"tracked\", but over the year they express appreciation for the extra help, pride in their successes, and comment that they can finally ask the questions that they want to without feeling dumb. We are continuing to follow these students to see if they can now keep up with their peers."

Many students view their education as a series of tasks to be completed, rather than a means of preparation for the tasks of adulthood; they quantify the point of termination rather than grasping the concept of lifelong learning. Thus, this mindset lowers the priority given education. They see lessons and courses as things to be completed; skills learned are not put fully to use because there is minimal pressure to do so. An analogy is learning to drive: You study a book of rules and regulations to pass a test for a learners permit, which is used to actually practice operating the vehicle. You then demonstrate highway and parking skills and are awarded a license. At that point, a marginal (knowledgable, yet unexperienced and potentially dangerous) driver joins all others. It is continued practice, full of trial and errors, that makes this person a more competent driver ... until old age begins reversing the process. The incentives for improvement driving skills are the likelihood of killing oneself or others, loss of driving privileges due accidents, and expense of vehicle repairs and medical costs. Math, like driving, is a series of skills that must be used (applied) in order to be retained, used successfully and improved.

Indeed there is not much difference. Let's look at the calculation of a population mean: Assumed, there are three possible events, A, B, C and you observe them na, nb, nc times. The population mean is (A*na + B*nb + C*nc)/nAgreed? This is equal to A*na/n + B*nb/n + C*nc/nNote, that na/n, nb/n and nc/n correspond to the experimental probabilities of A, B and C, and thus the population mean is equal to:A*P(A) + B*P(B) + C*P(C)Exactly the same thing. I would start the year by doing a very simple problem for the students and making a mistake on the problem. I will ask the students if they agree with my answer, if they can explain what I did, etc. If a student immediately recognizes my mistake, rather than say the correct answer to the class, I will have them come up and explain each step. As they get comfortable doing this, I would encourage the students to ask questions. If the students are comfortable and ask questions, the ideal situation would be for the whole class to discover the mistake and explain it. I would then ask the class questions about what I should do. Then I would emphasize that I made a mistake, and that is absolutely OK. I would emphasize that as we learn we make mistakes, and the mistakes make us even better learners and students. I would emphasize what I just learned from them helping me discover my mistake. I would also try to emphasize that mistakes are OK and to not be afraid of answering questions because we think we made a mistake.

Avotri and Walters’ piece “’We Women Worry A Lot About Our Husbands’” highlighted several key issues when assessing women’s health. They identify the problem that women’s health has almost been exclusively understood as synonymous with maternal or reproductive health, which is in fact only a small part of women’s biological lives. They then reason that this reproductive health agenda often comes from assumptions about women’s health, rather than actual needs assessments within women’s communities. It seems like such a simple step, but it just goes to show how blinkered health services have been that they wouldn’t think to ask women what health problems they are most concerned with. I think this failure to consult the women is but a further demonstration of the problems with Western and foreign aid, that, despite their best intentions, often end up speaking for communities and populations they know very little about, presuming, with a sort of buried ethnocentric mindset, that they know more than the communities they aim to help. One way that the authors could have elaborated further on this problem is by providing a follow-up study in which they consult the Ghanaian women they interviewed to find out how they think their psychosomatic ills and ailments can be ameliorated. I found the authors’ exploration of the psychosomatic symptoms the women suffer as a result of social, relational and economic stresses extremely interesting. Though international aid organizations often focus on the epidemics of communicable diseases, and acknowledge the role that socioeconomic status plays in vulnerability to illness, I haven’t read very much literature about how the stresses of living in an uncertain and unstable social and economic environment can impact the body. A woman may be suffering from a communicable disease, with the symptoms only further compounded by the stresses of making enough money to feed, clothe and educate her family, anxieties that can manifest into somatic disorders. The description the interlocutors give of headaches and loss of appetite also reminded me of the work that’s been done by anthropologists like Margaret Lock, who have investigated the concept of local biologies. However, transition words are highly, highly overused. ...I have students where I've edited almost every sentence starts with a transition word....I really want you to catch yourself if you find yourself reaching for too many transition words, that's probably indicating that you're overusingtransition words. ...the reason students tend to overuse them, is that they use them kind of as a crutch to make up for the fact that their logic isn't actually that sound. ...they're throwing in those transition words to try to kinda, you know, help fix the fact that their logic underneath is poor. But, that's not gonna fix it. You need to go back and fix the logic don't rely on those transition words. They're not, they're not strong enough to fix underlying logic that's not sound.So, use those sparingly. And, don't, don't be too exotic with your transition words either. You'll notice in a lot of professional writing, the favorite transition word is the word but, B, U, T. It's a great way to indicate to the reader, hey, but, I'm going to be changing gears here a little bit. So, you don't need fancy nevertheless, on the other hand, you can just use the word but, B, U, T.

For heavy users of tech, cloud storage is increasingly becoming a necessity for which they will pay a considerable price. However, many casual phone and tablet users have no need for cloud storage at high prices--the marginal benefit of even 1 GB of cloud storage simply isn't very high for them. Of course, these users would gravitate toward Google's free offering. Now, however, Google is making a play for slightly heavier users--people who could use more than 15GB but wouldn't pay much for it. Now, prices are going to be in line with marginal benefit for a larger number of consumers--increasing the quantity demanded. At the same time, heavy users--those who might already be willing to pay even at higher prices--will be able to achieve large consumer surpluses through Google's offering compared to another company's services. Thus, Google is both attracting new customers to the market ***and*** pulling the existing customers its way.

The hydration goal is to replace water and electrolyte losses ( i.e.,sodium, potassium,calcium,magnesium ) and correct volume contraction ( due to dehydration ).The nutrition goal is to replenish glycogen stores ( liver and muscle ) in preparation for the next bout of exercise and maintain/replace micro-nutrients required for optimal performance. The very important \ window \" of muscle-glycogen re-synthesis, which most experts have estimated within the first hour post exercise, is due to increased insulin sensitivity and hence, increased glucose and amino-acid influx across the muscle cell membrane. Carbohydrate consumption during this \"window of opportunity\", results in higher rates of glycogen storage. Also, liquid forms of carbohydrates provide extra fluids and are easy to digest and better tolerated, specially so, after exhaustive exercise. Many players opt to drop out because the possibility of making millions represents a significant opportunity cost of completing college.However, a case can be made for requiring all players to complete college before entering the NBA.Since only a handful of players who enter the NBA will eventually lead to highly illustrious careers, all but a very few of those who dropped out of college to play basketball will not achieve their greatest production potential (assuming that playing basketball represents their greatest talent.) On the contrary, it is more likely for most college graduates to be gainfully employed. While there are limited openings for the drafted players to compete for the multi-million dollar earning potentials, there are many more opportunities for college graduates to make millions by engaging in different sectors of the economy.If we were to plot the number of college graduates entering into the workforce on the x-axis and the number of NBA players (not completing college in order to play) on the y-axis, we would see the same curvature on the PPC shown earlier in our lecture. The more young men we have playing basketball instead of completing their college education, the higher the cost we'll incur in terms of adding more productive, educated workers to our workforce.Also, basketball players provide entertainment, hence a form of consumable. Consumption, as opposed to investment in the economy, does not help to shift out the production possibilities curve (aka the frontier.) Therefore, the second, possibly higher opportunity cost in this instance is the loss of greater progress of the economy as a whole.

The athlete's heart has intrigued generations of cardiologists, exercise physiologists, and basic research scientists. Cardiac and cardiovascular adaptations to high endurance exercise enhance the Inotropic (contractility), Lusitropic (relaxation), and modulate the Chronotropic (heart rate pacing) properties of the heart. Maximal endurance exercise is characterized by a 5 to 7+ fold increase in cardiac output,a 3 to 4 fold increase in heart rate, a 2 to 3 fold increase in stroke volume, and a 3 fold increase in the A-V Oxygen difference.High endurance training is characterized by an increase in both left and right cardiac chamber dimensions, wall thickness and mass. Transient mechanical dysfunction of the heart with simultaneous release of biomarkers ( troponins - small structural proteins of the muscle contraction apparatus ) has been documented in different types of athletes in rather heterogeneous populations ( marathon runners, soccer players, cyclist and others ).Why this happens is still unknown. The clinical significance remains a mystery.More importantly, both the short and long-term structural and physiologic adaptations or maladaptations of the heart based on the Dose-Respone stimulus is not known.Food for thought and research.

I have always liked the exercise in which a list of instructions, approximately 10 steps, is given. Number 1. is that you must read everything before you do anything. The students are always in a hurry to complete \everything,\" so they continue completing the rest of the steps until they arrive at step 10, which states that \"now that you have finished reading everything, write your name at the top of the paper.\" Inevitably, students have drawn circles and arrows all over their paper as instructed by the interim steps, only to find that the only writing on their paper should be their name! To discuss the feelings that went along with this very strong teaching moment, that it's not a race, are a wonderful start to growing a strong group of students who can work together, and who are vulnerable to making mistakes; which is exactly what we want! Almost certainly, most students will have made the mistake and everybody then has the chance to start again, fresh."

You need to read the help for `?cv.glm` closely. Note the argument `cost`, which has the following description:> `cost`: A function of two vector arguments specifying the cost function for the cross-validation. The first argument to `cost` should correspond to the observed responses and the second argument should correspond to the predicted or fitted responses from the generalized linear model. `cost` must return a non-negative scalar value. **The default is the average squared error function.**(Emphasis mine). The highlight bit is important; by default `cv.glm()` is computing the MSE of the predictions under CV and you are computing the error rate. You are using a different, but more appropriate, cost function compared to the default in `cv.glm()`.The solution is actually given in the **Examples** section of `?cv.glm`; supply to the `cost` argument a suitable cost function, such as the one in the last example: # ... Since the response is a binary variable an # appropriate cost function is cost <- function(r, pi = 0) mean(abs(r-pi) > 0.5)Then if you proceed with cv.err <- cv.glm(Weekly, glmfit, cost = cost)You get the same LOOCV error as you computed by hand: R> cv.err$delta [1] 0.4500 0.4501----If you are interested in learning more about writing R code etc, you could make your code considerably more efficient by stopping after the prediction step in the lop, and save each predicted value, and then do the later steps on the entire data set. E.g. pred <- numeric(length = nrow(Weekly)) #loop thru the cases for (i in 1:nrow(Weekly)){ #fit a glm for all but case i butone <- glm(Direction ~ Lag1 + Lag2, data = Weekly[-i,], family=binomial) # use this glm to calc a probability of point i pred[i] <- predict(butone, newdata=Weekly[i,], type=\response\") } # calculate average error rate rate <- sum((pred > 0.5) + 1 != as.numeric(Weekly$Direction)) / nrow(Weekly)The reason this can be more efficient is that we can do all the comparisons of the predicted values in a single vectorised shot (and I've employed a bit of jiggery-pokery to simplify those steps into a single line of code), whereas your version was calling `ifelse()` 2178 times (`nrow(Weekly) * 2`), plus `+` 1089 times. Each of these has an overhead so it helps to only do in the loop "

The difference between Q2 and Q3 lies in which predictors you are using to fit the OLS model (the response remains the same, the y).In Q2, you are using 5 predictors that correspond to 5 orthogonal linear combinations of the original 200 predictors (you obtain them by projecting the original data onto the first 5 principal components that PCA gave you using the predict function) while in Q3, you are using 200 predictors (the original ones).In terms of R code, the model fitting should look the same (`lm(y ~ ., data=train)`) but the data frame `train` should be different between Q2 and Q3. One uses the original x and y while the other uses the projected x over the first 5 principal components and y.Note that indeed I did not get the expected answers but mine still fell within the error margin.

I get calls from parents asking for the source of my material so students can practice ahead of time to make class easier. I tell them I write the material and they are missing the point. I want my students to struggle and think because that is when they learn. I ask them to be patient and trust me. They usually do. When a senior in my class is soon to graduate, I always inquire, \What next?\" When students are headed to a 4-year college, they are excited to leave home, room with friends, and start the next stage of their life. When a student is going to a 2-year college, most have yet to enroll before the year is up, must figure out how to pay for courses, and many use the word ™probablyš in their response (as in, ™I®ll probably go to PCC in the fallˆš). In these conversations, I®ve found that the general 2-year college student is not as committed to the idea of taking the next step as their 4-year counterpart. I ask what drives them or what is their path of interest, and most 2-year students are unsure and say they are going to ™figure it out when they get thereš whereas the 4-year student has determined the general field they will pursue at least to get started. Contributing factors that I always wonder about after these conversations include how much guidance a student has received from a parent/mentor/counselor, the student®s ability to make decisions and have confidence in those decisions (I could not make these decisions myself but had parent support), and if there is the presence of a positive peer group that has influence over their life®s direction. We need to build a better bridge to connect the student to the field of interest. This takes me back to the common thread that teachers must do a better job connecting math concepts with their application beyond the classroom and though this has been stated a hundred times and is essential, I believe a student must see themselves personally invested/directly linked to that career path. We need to make available more work study programs, and require a student to commit to one over a long period of time. Experience in the field will build confidence and give students more direction allowing for full commitment in whatever path they follow.When forced to choose between choices a-d (see I can make decisions now), I would choose b) Lack of Foundational Knowledge, followed by a) The Teaching Methods. Rent control has been used to protect tenants from landlords and investors looking to turnover renters and potentially sell their investments (in this case property) at higher prices. In San Francisco's case, breaking the rent control policy will help landowners and landlords provide an incentive to investors to buy more apartments and condos, turning over the property into my affluent hands. This would potentially increase property value as well. The consequences of this policy is the dire situation that may happen when the policy changes and rent is no longer controlled. When the policy changes, those tenants will most likely be evicted or face steep rent increases. Also, these populations will most likely have to move further from their city, either in the outskirts or perhaps pushed to suburbs. Any number of 'opportunity' costs could be associated with such a move. There is also a cultural cost. Communities that typically make less money are pushed out, like art and theater, and diversity in community is also more difficult to maintain if only the affluent can afford to live there.I have lived in cities where rent control and high rent costs have become an issue. As rent prices get higher in areas that gentrify, residents that earn lower wages or have less income get priced out and have to leave the neighborhood. Then they move to a cheaper neighborhood, pricing out those that have lived there. And this cycle continues until those that have the lowest incomes are priced out of the city altogether or find themselves living in smaller quarters with more people.

Ah, the frequentist vs. bayesian interpretations of confidence intervals!Short answer: neither interpretations suggest a 95% probability of truth for the parameter.Long answer: read on.For the frequentist, there is only one true value for the parameter, which is hidden and we can only approximate based on theories like the Central Limit Theorem. Note that with each random sample set, we expect to get different confidence intervals. Hence, the 95% confidence interval for a parameter indicates that if we repeated the fitting using multiple (& infinite) number of random sample sets, 95% of confidence intervals produced would contain the true parameter. For the bayesian, the parameter (or its estimate) is random and has an associated probability distribution. The 95% **credible** interval communicates the level of belief the experimenter has of the parameter i.e. she is 95% sure of the parameter estimate. Note that it is a subjective belief as the interval is of the posterior distribution based on the experimenter's chosen prior. So, if you're not confused by now, you haven't understood it. In practise, most decisions are made as you suggested: 95% probability the true parameter lies within the interval. Nothing bad has happened yet, well maybe the GFC.

It seems to me that with complex issues such as correlation between minimum wage and unemployment, it is overly-simplified to claim that higher minimum wage will always cause unemployment among lower-income workers. My first question is if it is a real increase or just adjustment to inflation? Question that maybe is not the most relevant from the supply-demand model point of view, but good for a bigger point of view. Companies tend to adjust their prices to inflation or use the inflation to give them the \competitive edge\". But the latter on the expense of the employers whose salaries cannot be adjusted without threatening the profits. In long term i cannot see how this can be good for the economy nor do I believe that employers, who in essence have lower negotiation power, should pay for this \"competitive edge\". If the companies are just \"playing the system\" it seems to me that equilibrium is kept unnaturally low anyways and increase buts it back to real equilibrium. Majority of companies reliant on low-skilled employers will not reduce their profits for the well being of their employees, unless they have to. So, assuming it is the real increase of wages, the questions is how capable is the company to pass the extra costs on to consumers (competitiveness issue). Or how sensitive is their stock to decreases in profits. But if majority of goods and services are provided by lower-income workers then passing on the costs could, I assume, cause inflation. There are also other factors, such as the general environment/policies of the country and its social security net, including capacity to train its workforce to respond to demands of the market. From this point of view, raising the minimum wage could have completely different results depending on the country due to the deepness and shifts in the curves specific to that particular country. So it seems to be, that it all depends on numerous factors - raise in minimum wage could actually raise the cost of the products or increase unemployment, or nothing of the before mentioned would happen. But i do assume that companies will have better economists than employers to argue for the final and absolute truth."

Rajhansa's definition can be more generalized to state that any variable where there are 'many' categories can be treated as continuous - it doesn't have to be very large (eg. all decimal numbers between 0-1 form a continuous range of numbers). How many is 'many' could be debatable. Perhaps what's more important is that to treat a discrete variable as continuous in a way that makes sense, it should be ordinal and the difference between adjacent classes should be identical in value. Eg. even though the age groupings (0-10, 10-20, 20-30, ... ) and (0-10, 10-30, 30-40, ...) are discrete, the former can be more easily treated as continuous than the latter.

The Economist article on Europe's electricity providers facing an existential threat talks about the situation being faced by them in short run as well as in long run.While the traditional power plants using coal and gas have high fixed cost as well as high operating costs, the solar and wind power generation is more capital intensive and is having very minimal cost of operation. Hence the marginal cost for the traditional power plants is very high and the solar and wind power plants have zero marginal cost. As more and more power is generated from Solar and Wind power plants as compared to the traditional power plants, the prices of wholesale price is decreasing and with decreasing prices the traditional power plants face a situation where price (P) is less than Average Variable cost (AVC) i.e. P< AVC and hence the companies are loosing on the revenues and face a situation where in short run the companies will be loosing money and if the situation persist the firms face a shut down conditions. as mentioned in certain situations , like on June 16th the traditional power companies have to pay to supply the power to the grid, hence in such situations the companies would be forced to not produce any output and hence will be in a loss making situation as the fixed cost of the plants will not be recovered.The article also shows how the utility wholesale prices have dropped down in last three years and because of this the margins of traditional power plants have come down to a point where they would have to look at shutting down the plants.If this situation persists then in long run many of the traditional power plants will exit the business or many of these companies will look at getting into the renewable energy plants.

There is one problem I have with this issue. We are going to track students. You are not going to have kindergarten students in the same math class with high school students. We are going to draw lines and group students according to some criterion. Why does it make more sense to do it by age than by ability? When I take yoga, I go to a beginner class, because if I go to the advanced class I will get hurt. And the advanced students would be bored if they had to take class with me. It would be an additional burden and water down the class if we required all yoga teachers to teach to all levels in the same class. It just doesn't make sense. Clearly ability grouping in math does impact students in all sorts of negative ways. But is this more a product of the culture this video describes than the actual logic of having different levels? What would we say about someone who said I am not going to take piano because my friend is in the fourth book and my teacher wants me to start in the first book? We need to provide quality material at all levels and teach students to divorce their self-image from whatever level they are currently (applies to a lot of adults too), not trade age grouping for ability grouping.

If you're in a situation where you are likely to develop hypothermia, the key thing is to have a plan for it, and doggedly stick to the plan. If possible, set things up so that your plan is very easy to follow.In winter swimming, mild hypothermia symptoms are pretty common, so you get used to dealing with yourself and others in a bit of a state. So you plan to get out, get dressed as quick as possible, get somewhere warm or wrapped up, out of the wind, eat sugary food and warm drinks. When you're getting in, you lay out your clothes, changing towel first (big hooded towel you stand inside and change), then underwear and trousers, then socks and shoes, then t-shirt, jumper, windproof coat, flask of hot chocolate, piece of cake. When you get out, even if you're a bit confused and your eyes are fuzzy, everything is there for you in order, the easiest thing to do is to follow the plan.It's still best to be with other people if possible, nothing helps more than having a helpful person to make sure you're putting your clothes on the right way round, or to make you a cup of tea or whatever.

Is GDP a fair and accurate representation of national health? That depends on what is meant by \national health.\" By national health do we mean we are happier, have less problems such as sudden infant deaths, chronic diseases, and less crime? If this is what is meant by national health, then GDP is a poor measure.Even when we talk about economic health, GDP is found wanting. GDP is suppose to be the value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders over some period of time, but it does not adjust for the value of economic resources that have been destroyed, not just depleted. The Great Plains have a season in which tornadoes strike and destroy homes and other structures, not to mention injury to people and loss of human life. The same with hurricane season on the East and Gulf Coasts. After such catastrophes, people rebuild and this rebuilding is counted toward GDP, but the value of the economic resources that were destroyed or made unusable is not factored into this measure of the \"health of the economy.\"Realizing that any measure of how a country is doing will have its flaws, I would suggest that besides GDP we include a measure of economic losses for the period to offset the value lost in such things as catastrophes. The measure might also include some human factors such as a measure of income inequality knowing that the economy cannot remain robust over the long term if a great proportion of the population is not receiving a portion of the gains from a growing economy. Thus some \"national progress index\" would supplement GDP with a measure of economic losses due to accidents, natural disasters and other catastrophes. Property insurance claims might be used as one estimate for such losses. Another would be an estimate of the economic value that could have been created had there been less income inequality. Thus something like the Gini index may be employed, but selecting a \"desirable\" level to represent a preferable income distribution to be achieved is open to controversy and is unlikely to gain universal agreement.Americans have one of the highest per capita income levels in the world, but in one survey they also were said to be among the angriest people in the world. Contrast this with some of the poorest regions on the continent of Africa and you will find, despite their poverty, that they are content, even happy, with their lot. Perhaps the old saying is true: Money can't buy happiness."

What currently grabs my interest are the posts regarding roles kids take in groups - be it ability grouping or mixed ability. How can we ensure all kids get a chance to participate and be a leader? I work as a substitute teacher and have - in the same district - for three years now. I go into many classrooms and all have some degree of grouping/tracking. Math is usually grouped by whole classes - with several classes being offered. I see far more ability grouping in reading. I dread stepping into these groups because they are lifeless and repetitive and do not offer any authentic learning.

As a student I always felt grouping was a form of labelling and creating a caste system within a classroom. YEs I was the highest reader but that label set me up as an outcast with my peers so I tried to appear less interested in reading and to answer less of the reading comprehension questions correctly in the SRA reading system, simply to fit in. I believe students desire to fit in first to feel safe in order to feel accepted and then and only then are they free to focus on learning. In maths I was in the lowest grouping because I had missed basics being prematurely promoted to 6th grade in Canada (mislabeled) as I immigrated from England and the education systems when compared by my teachers in Canada appeared to be ahead in England. My language and literacy scores supported this theory however had I been properly and fully assessed in maths it would of been obvious that there were gaps and lags in basic skills for multiple reasons. Math phobia, I believed the girl messages about math and tuned math out more than I tuned in to cope with maths class. However I can see now and look back to see that without proper knowledge of my ability I was mis placed and a border casualty in being improperly grouped and placed. Both groups damaged me as a student. The system of teaching these subjects was also quite independent in both systems in the 70's and 80's many of my teachers sat behind desks while we worked quietly at our own desk. Discussion was not encouraged and a lot of the work, in literacy I found to be not challenging. Math's I found it to be too challenging and without support I gave up.As a teacher I place students in mixed ability Tribe groups for all subjects. Tribe leaders rotate and are not based on ability in the subject but on group communication leadership skills. there is a leader and a leader in training each week. All students have a voice in these learning groups and all questions, opinions and strategies are respected, explored and explained by members of the Tribe group. I have personally seen students with IEP's 2 grades below flourish and thrive beyond mine, their parents and their own expectations of their learning. They are supported, cheered and encouraged by their peers for the smallest of gains. this celebration of persisting at tasks is the focus not getting it right the first time. All students in the mixed ability groupings consider a variety of strategies and ways of knowing and this I believe is the key to strong students con

Ah, yes. The reality is that applying these techniques when in the whirlwind of real life emotion is super challenging! I like to consider them a fun (or sometimes not so fun) challenge, even when I fail or it takes me longer to succeed. For example, today, my video editing software kept crashing on my computer. In the scheme of things, probably not a big deal, but I'm on a deadline and it was really frustrating me! By the third time it crashed, my blood was boiling and half a day had been wasted. I was ANGRY! In the heat of that emotion, it took me some time before I realized what was going on, and that in this case, continuing to stress out wasn't going to help me make my computer work better. So it wasn't until I had already been riding an emotional wave for a while before I realized that I could make a few different choices that would help me out. In this case, I used Freeze Frame and gave my significant other a call. Bottom line is, certainly isn't always pretty, but with some practice new choices start opening up that can add value in tough situations. Lots of people before me have already posted very eloquently on the problems inherent in math classrooms. I believe that many students don't know how to problem solve and lack the know-how or motivation to keep on going when they hit a stumbling block. Many students just want the answer and the correct sequence for how that answer was derived without really understanding (or caring) about the WHYS of mathematical thinking. Perhaps this most closely relates to choice A given in the multiple choice items. But I don't know if this is the most prevalent problem, really. I think too that students lack a fundamental level of confidence in their abilities to think and reason and learn. If that confidence can be built up, then successes can be experienced and progress made. I've always said that my greatest challenge as an instructor is to teach my students how to learn in general (regardless of content area) - to teach them how to seek out answers, to question, to be curious and use tools (such as technology) to help themselves. A lot of students today just don't persevere in the face of a challenge. That's our challenge though as instructors - to try to help our students combat and conquer that mindset.

Great question John. I'll do my best to do a quick and dirty overview of metabolism in a short note. Basically, your body has access to three main energy systems to help you do work. 1) Immediate energy (ATP, creatine-phosphate) 2) Short term energy (fast glycolysis) and 3) Long term energy (oxidative, or oxygen using, metabolism). Energy systems 1 and 2 can be done without oxygen, and system 3 needs oxygen. Your body is using some combination of these three energy systems at any given time, but will rely more heavily on a particular system depending on the situation. The general theme relevant here is that the body transitions from relying on oxidative metabolism to relying on non oxidative metabolism as exercise intensity increases. To make this a little more practical, \going anaerobic\" means higher intensity exercise. If you find yourself breathing heavily (even if its not for very long!), you are likely dipping in to nonoxidative metabolism aka \"going anaerobic\". -staff"

I think math is a problem for many students in the US because of the teaching methods taught in schools and colleges. Classes don't always allow for students to process the information learned at their own pace. Often, time is not allowed for students to think through the information given on their own, to ask themselves whether their process or the answer makes sense, nor do they have time to discuss the information with others. I believe students need to develop strategies to preview the information given, to understand what is being asked of them in the question or prompt, and to be able to go through a process to problem solve. The process of solving the problem is more important than the final answer. Time should also be given to discuss the problem with others. I think in high school, teachers feel pressure to get through their curriculum and instead of helping students develop problem solving strategies and ways to recognize and look at different problems, some instructors just given the answers and students think their job is to just copy notes or copy work from the board.

I've studied this phenomenon in Armenia, where I am currently based. I have found that, though several women cite sex preference, few perceive that a sex-selective abortion is necessary for them. The interplay of familial and socio-cultural influences highly skews decision-making regarding abortion in the event that husbands and/or the husband’s family exhibit son preference. The overwhelming majority of women are either outwardly pressured by family members or feel a duty to carry out sex-selective abortions for their families. Women note that their husbands desire to have sons due to a number of intertwined factors based on tradition, such as the perception that only sons continue the family lineage and national heritage; obtain the family inheritance; provide security to aging parents; and are a source of physical strength needed for the family and to defend the homeland. Along with familial influence and traditional mentality, factors such as the poor living conditions affect the desire to have fewer children and children of both sexes. I'd be happy to send my thesis and other resources to anyone who's interested in learning more about this phenomenon in the Armenian context.

Gene Richardson's talks were really interesting and valuable. I was previously aware of social interventions but had not heard them described in such an articualted way. So this was really valuable.Social interventions, to be effective, need to empower girls and boys, men and women to act responsibly and with respect for the long term. The interventions I'll describe below try to focus on this idea; how can we empower girls to demand respect, and how can we empower boys to give that respect without question?Firstly, as we saw in week two, education is one of the best ways to teach young girls (and boys) their value and potential. In a couple of the readings, \low self-esteem\" is identified as a risk factor for contracting HIV. It therefore points to the need all girls have to be supported and listened to - school education forms an opportunity for this. If a girl feels her point of view and she as a person are respected and valued by her classmates, then she might be more confident in navigating contraceptive talks with her sexual partner, and demanding the use of condoms or tests. Of course education can also give girls the tools to know both how HIV is transmitted, and how to avoid being infected.Secondly, it seems a lot of women are at risk throughout the world because of their financial dependence on men. Whether that be relying on a \"sugar daddy\" to pay the rent or an unfaithful husband to buy the food. NGOs, councils, government bodies, multilaterals etc need to focus their resources on supporting women to create their own financial independence. KIVA loans and microfinance are a good example here. This financial independence could help some women escape from their dependence on men who carry the virus and/or behave in a risky way.Lastly, it's crucial that whatever structural intervention is put in place for girls and women is also explained and sold to boys and men. Boys and men need to also learn, understand and accept the value of each girl and woman, and her point of view in the context of safe respectful sex and HIV/AIDS. Respected male leaders could be recruited from relevant communities to educate their peers and lead by example."

I don't think \all hope is lost\", it is just that the interpretation of the coefficients in the case of multiple covariates that are correlated becomes more difficult. Hence in that figure, subsets regression and the LASSO are consider more interpretable than the OLS method, as these methods aim to remove some of the predictors (or reduce their effects) giving fewer \"things\" to consider at once.There are ways to help one interpret the effect on $y$ of changes in the covariates $X$, such as partial plots, where you hold all bar 1 or 2 of the covariates at some value, vary the values of the 1 or 2 covariates not held fixed and draw the change in the response as you vary them. Of course, if there are correlations or interactions, the effect of the covariates you are varying can change with the values of the other covariates you held fixed. Hence you might repeat this exercise, say using the lower quartile, median and upper quartile values to fix covariates at.The point is not to give up and not interpret the meaning of the coefficient estimates, but to critically think through what they *actually* mean. Yes, this gets more difficult with many correlated or interacting predictors, but no-one said doing stats was easy or quick :-)"

**Why do so many players play one year of basketball in college then forgo the remainder of their college career to enter the NBA?**Firstly, entering the NBA league is prestigious and highly coveted, given the league's reputation worldwide and the most successful basketball players like Michael Jordan and Kobe Bryan were from NBA. Because of this **demand**, hence students are willing to give up college to enter NBA. To the promising young basketball player who wants to build his basketball career, the opportunity cost of going to college and not playing in NBA is significantly higher than the opportunity cost of dropping out and playing in the NBA. In order to make a decision that is best aligned to his/her ambitions and has the lowest opportunity cost, hence it is understandable why dropout occurs. **No Restriction or Increase the Limit? **The restriction should be dropped altogether, because the opportunity cost of going to college and subsequently dropping out is more substantial than the opportunity cost of not going to college altogether and entering the NBA right after high school. For the individual himself/herself, college is not as ideal a training ground for basketball specific skills as compared to the NBA. Given that a NBA career places an extremely high premium on basketball skills (e.g. dribbling, passing, etc.) rather than skills and knowledge picked up from college classes, the one year in college before the individual drops out has a high opportunity cost (that is, not being able to focus 100% on playing basketball as he would have in NBA).If the individual can play basketball right after high school in NBA, the opportunity cost is a college education. However, this is a lower opportunity cost than one year of college (with less rigorous basketball-training), because the student desires to pursue a career in NBA. Entering NBA right after high school means no time lost from spending one year in college, hence lower opportunity cost.
