The interview with Gene Richardson tackles what I believe to be imperative when tackling HIV/AIDS prevention and treatment, particularly as we’re aiming to approach these issues through the lens of human rights. Similar to the education argument—more girls in schools means more girls in the workplace means a better GDP means a better world—taking a look at a disease so fully entrenched in additional contexts through the sole lens of medicine can be problematic. When considering social and/or non-medical interventions that might be effective in preventing or treating HIV/AIDS, it’s a different kind of chicken-before-the-egg problem. As we’ve been discussing throughout this course, many of the issues women across the globe are facing are closely intertwined with larger issues and broader social/economic/political contexts. I mention this only as a reminder—for myself and for the reader—of the boundaries and challenges my recommendations face. My recommendations all stem from education: 1.) improved access and education quality for girls and 2.) improved sex education for both boys and girls. Improving access to and quality of education for girls has a number of possible benefits. Over the last two weeks, we’ve seen studies and readings mention time and time again that the more educated girls are, the less likely they are to marry later and to practice safe sex. Not to mention educated girls are more likely to be personally empowered, gain respect in the community and have more opportunities to pursue a healthier economic situation. All of these can help reduce their chances of contracting HIV/AIDS. While it’s clear to see the correlation between marrying later and practicing safe sex, I think the latter points are most interesting and pertinent to prevention and treatment. Ideally, the more empowered girls are around the world and the more respected they are in their communities, the less likely they are to be entrenched in extreme poverty—where many risk factors lie. Of course, it’s important to remember how they’re receiving this education and the various risk factors of sexual favors and debt bondage to receive access to schooling. In addition to improving education across the board, I am a huge advocate that improved sex education can greatly influence HIV/AIDS prevention. Sam has some great ideas and describes these with excellent graphics on the board. I truly believe the lesson could have been moved forward if the students had been asked to write down an example of a division that they did know the answer to and to then explain how they know their answer is correct. Students should then be asked to use this knowledge about 'simpler' division to help solve the 'harder' problem (i.e. the focus of this lesson). This is not the place to develop the lesson further but if students cannot use their prior knowledge of 'easier' division then they have to be told to look for an alternate 'reason' for their 'easy' division answer being correct.Once some understanding is gained the next step should be to start recording answers on the board and looking for patterns in the answers and from this, if sensible, a method developed... by the students.And here is where the real detailed lesson planning becomes important because it is these follow up questions that have to be accessible and useful in finding patterns that lead to a method. None that I’ve seen include a practical measure of uncertainty.Every economic decision involves forecasting the future. And the future is always uncertain. I tried to specify some specific uncertainties but I discovered there are far too many to list. The economic models shown can’t possibly reflect reality because they don’t include uncertainty!And, worse, I’ve never seen an economic model, including my own, that included a reasonable measure of uncertainty. My economic models tell me what might happen, but never give a reasonable estimate of the probability of occurrence. I always end up using numbers as words. If I tell management there is an 80% chance of a venture succeeding, I’m expressing a level of confidence, an opinion, not an actual estimate of probability.Contrast this with statistical quality control where I can determine whether variability is predictable with time, and if it is I can give my management a very a good estimate of the nature and probability of defectives.Life just ain't fair!

Remember, we're looking for the beta that minimizes RSS. Now, let's say I add one more game, game n+1, which is played between Weber State (let's call them team 1) and some new team, team p+1. The claim is that (beta hat 1, ..., beta hat p) are going to be the same before and after I add this data point.Here's why: for *any* setting of beta hat 1, I can set beta hat (p+1) so my model perfectly predicts game n+1. Because beta hat (p+1) only affects one game, that is exactly what I'll do.But then, minimizing RSS over all n+1 games just reduces to minimizing RSS over the first n games, and then choosing beta hat (p+1) to make the last residual 0.

This question asks us to come up with the LOOCV error rate by \brute force\" instead of using the `cv.glm()` function. I came up with an answer that's vastly different using this method. Was wondering what went wrong. Here's my R code for convenience:count=rep(1:nrow(Weekly),0)for (i in 1:nrow(Weekly)) {glm.fit.loo = glm(Direction~Lag1+Lag2,data=Weekly,family=binomial,subset = -i)count[i]=ifelse(ifelse(predict(glm.fit.loo,Weekly[i,])>0.5,\"Up\",\"Down\")==Weekly[i,]`$`Direction,1,0)}Here's the R code for getting the LOOCV error from above (which turns out to be 0.545):1-sum(count)/nrow(Weekly)And here's the R code for getting the LOOCV error by using `cv.glm()` (which turns out to be 0.246):glm.fit.all = glm(Direction~Lag1+Lag2,data=Weekly,family=binomial)cv.glm(Weekly,glm.fit.all)`$`deltaWhy is there such a big discrepancy?"

In working with students on such problems I abandon the goal of representing the situation in one variable and let them use A, B, and C to start with. I don't see a need to use just one variable, and having 3 variables allows the students to keep the original problem in their head a little longer in working the problem. Jumping right to using 'x' to show the relationship between the three piles is too big a step. So: B = A + 2; C = 4A; A + B + C = 14. Then I talk about the importance of the equals sign. The equals sign is a like a balance scale. The equals sign means you have the same amount of stuff on the left that you do on the right. The equals sign means that the thing on the left (same amount of stuff as the right) can be substituted for the thing on the right anywhere it appears in this problem. And vise versa -- the thing on the right can be substituted for the thing on the left anywhere it appears in the problem. So in this case, you can substitute A + 2 for B anywhere it appears in this situation, and 4A for C anywhere it appears in this situation. So A + B + C = 14 can become A + (A + 2) + (4A) = 14. I will actually write out A + B + C = 14 with lots of space, and then erase the B and write \(A + 2)\" in its place; erase C and write \"(4A)\" in its place. At this point I will point out we now have a mathematical representation of the problem of piles of rocks and how many is in each pile. We check that this mathematical representation truly does represent the problem. Then I ask them for a leap of faith -- to not think about the original problem, but just look at the equation. Now there is one variable in the equation and let's find out what it is -- view it as a puzzle. I let them do any manipulation they want to as long as that manipulation honors the equals sign. We look at the result of each manipulation, asking does it make the equation look less complex -- always keeping in mind that at this point, the goal is to get to \"A = ...\" -- finding out what A is. Once we have A, I have the student look back at the original problem and tell me what we've found out. I ask have we answered the question originally asked? If a single variable, such as x is used, it is easy to think the problem is solved once we find x. But by using A, and looking back at the original problem, we find that we know A, but there is still more work to do to find B and C."

Let's say you have a high test error and you would like to reduce it.So you should compare test error with train error.1. If train error is high, you have high bias. It is also known as underfitting. It means you are using too simple (not flexible) model. So you should try to use more complex (flexible) model. For example, you have high bias if you are trying to fit quadratic function with linear regression.2. If train error is low, then you have high variance. It is also known as overfitting. It means you are using too complex (flexible) model. So you should try to use more simple (not flexible) model. Also, you should definitely have a look at https://class.coursera.org/ml-003/lecture/62

You idea of \tailing off\" is a good way how to explain by logic not only the demand curve, but the supply curve as well. If a business owner or manufacturer sees an opportunity to sell for a higher price, he is incentivised by the price increase to produce and sell as much as possible. But since the resources (employees, premises, etc. factors of production) is limited business owner / manufacturer by the price increase tries to optimise and get the most out of the resources it has. So every time when price increases driven by innovation / efficiency / economy of scale / creativity it figures out how to supply even more at the price. The curve \"tails off\" when the production resources is close to its end and it is impossible to produce even more units than before because of the production possibility curve."

I think that Pareto efficiency is misleading because it can be taken to imply that everybody has what they want. Actually, the situation where Thomas had both books was also Pareto efficient as he was worse off by giving one back to Tilky. A situation where all the wealth is in the hands of one person is also Pareto efficient, because that person will be worse off if (s)he gives a pint of milk to one other person. Efficiency is not the same as fairness.

If you are referring to the standard error or the difference as in the difference of two means, that's not the same thing as this problem. In this problem you take each subjects before and after scores, compute the difference between their before and after scores and then average those differences. In the example you are talking about above, we would take the average for all of the first scores, then the average for all of the second scores and then find the differences of those two averages. That is an entirely different creature mathematically, if you had the individual scores you could compute either but, if you do not have the individual scores, you cannot compute what we want using either the two averages and SDs or the pooled SD. It's not in general possible.

I teach in the UK to 11 to 16 yr olds. I have been at this same school for 7 years. Every year group is set by 'ability'. This is basically decided on by the test they have at the end of the year before but we also give a teacher assessment and base this on work in class and homework. My Head of Department (HOD) is very reasonable and if we can justify where we think a student should go, then he is willing to listen and often moves that student on our advice. I think the reason why we set the year groups is to make teachers lives easier. It is a hard enough job anyway, with OFSTED (government inspectors) rating the school and it's teachers on a scale of 1 to 4. If you are rated as a 3, you are satisfactory but you are made to feel that that is not good enough. Likewise if you are graded a 2, good, you feel you should be achieving a 1, outstanding. Having to fully differentiate every lesson for every class and provide the necessary resources is exhausting. If there was a bank of resources made for every lesson, suitable for every child, it would be possible but there are just not enough hours in the day to create/adapt lessons for everything you could possible need. And an exhausted teacher is no good to anyone. I think it will take a long time to move forward unless more teachers/schools/countries are willing to and have the time to share their good resources. This year we are trying an experiment where we are teaching our year 9s as mixed ability except for the 'top' set. We have had a lot of comments from parents and my school is very driven by them. My HOD has had to write many letters explaining why we are trying out this way of teaching. Parents will need to be educated in this way if we are to move forward in this respect. I cannot see how giving the higher achievers the message \you're smart\" can be damaging to them. Surely by letting them see their own achievements they believe more in themselves and then we tell them that we expect even more from them and give them the most challenging of problems but still explain to them that part of progressing is to make mistakes to learn from them and then to push themselves harder."

For those interested in trying this out for themselves, here's some R code for the plots above.(Hopefully it's self contained, so you can justcut-and-paste to get started)First plot (not perfect separation) data1 <- data.frame(x=c(1,2,3,4,5,6), y=c(0,0,1,0,1,1)) plot(y ~ x, data=data1) glm.fit <- glm(y ~ x , family=\binomial\", data=data1) glm.fit b0 <- glm.fit$coefficients[1] b1 <- glm.fit$coefficients[2] prob.curve.data <- data.frame(x=seq(1,6,by=0.01)) prob.curve.data <- transform(prob.curve.data, prob=exp(b0+b1*x)/(1+exp(b0+b1*x))) plot(y ~ x, data=data1) lines(prob ~ x, data=prob.curve.data, col=\"blue\") Second plot (perfect separation) data2 <- data.frame(x=c(1,2,3,4,5,6), y=c(0,0,0,1,1,1)) plot(y ~ x, data=data2) glm.fit <- glm(y ~ x , family=\"binomial\", data=data2) glm.fit b0 <- glm.fit$coefficients[1] b1 <- glm.fit$coefficients[2] prob.curve.data <- data.frame(x=seq(1,6,by=0.01)) prob.curve.data <- transform(prob.curve.data, prob=exp(b0+b1*x)/(1+exp(b0+b1*x))) plot(y ~ x, data=data2) lines(prob ~ x, data=prob.curve.data, col=\"red\")"

\predict\" is what is known as a \"generic function\" in R, which means that it dispatches different methods depending upon the class of the object that is passed to the function.For example, if you pass a linear model to the predict function, it dispatches the method predict.lm to actually perform the prediction. Or if you pass a logistic regression model to the predict function, it dispatches the method predict.glm.If you run the following line of code in R, it will show you all of the methods for predict: methods(predict)And then once you know the method's name, you can get help on that method.If the line of code above does not work for a particular function, you can also try: showMethods(\"insert_function_name_here\")The methods() call works for what are called \"S3 functions\", whereas the showMethods() call works for \"S4 functions\".-<nameRedac_<anon_screen_name_redacted>>"

Cathy set the task then allowed the students to respond. She gave them time- one minute?- to begin their exploration. Two different answers came up. On bringing the class together she asked for an explanation of the two answers. The reasons given were attempts to uncover a rule. At this stage, she stopped them told them that before the end of the class they would have a rule and altered the task to can you make sense of the question/solution. The atmosphere in the class accepted this, there were no visual signs of frustration. Cathy used the language of \yes, but does it make sense?\" throughout the discussion, never hinting at 'the correctness' of each of the three methods shared. She expected the class to criticise the speaker introducing the roles, asking each to be friendly or sceptical. The 10 minutes were spent reinforcing the mathematical methods of representing problems in different ways, supporting your answers with reasons, analysing other ideas,checking methods through inverse operations; the learning process of re-explaining another point of view and the social learning of collaboration. Only after these processes had been explored was a traditional method introduced."

I think that dot card number talk breaks the strict linear limits that most people have been taught in mathematics and lets them actually \see\" math in more creative, more individual ways -- almost artistically. For me, it is almost like asking people to tell you what they see when they gaze at stars in the sky -- you wouldn't expect strict and absolute answers in how someone \"sees\" something like that in their mind -- just in the same way, how very differently they represent the dots on the card in ways that make it \"legible\" to them. In this case, they are being asked specifically to \"see\" the math answer and I think just watching how the different students do that -- and all arrive still at the \"correct\" answer is amazingly enjoyable in telling everyone that there is no one right, predetermined \"intelligent\" way -- there are many, many ways to see and get to the correct answer.I think that can open a math class up to a whole new level of excitement and willingness to engage!"

*Traditionally*, academic papers were written in the third person and passive voice (as we have seen). That implies that, where it was essential for the authors to refer to themselves it was done via the circumlocution of 'the researchers...' For example, 'The participants were observed covertly by the researcher...' even where the research was carried out and written up by an individual (and thus the writer was referring to him- or herself).This style still seems common in many UK journals although, from Kristin's comments, that may be less so in the US. In that case the extract might be re-written, 'I covertly observed the participants...' using the first person and an active voice.I'd suggest that, in the absence of a formal style guide, previous (recent) material in the journal might provide a fair indication of expectations.

I ran 100 trials using the NYT interactive feature and it turns out math works. In each of the trials I elected to switch doors and it resulted in my winning the car 67% of the time. Conceptually, it helped to think about this problem by virtue of negation, i.e. in which of the possible scenarios would I NOT want to switch. The answer is one in three (the one in which I chose the car straight away), therefore, conversely, I would want to switch in two out of three scenarios.I had another way of approaching the problem in which I simply thought about choosing 'new' doors. In the first round of choosing, I had a 33% chance of choosing a new door with a car behind it and a 66% chance of choosing a new door with a goat behind it. Once a goat was revealed, I only had one 'new' door to select and my odds of selecting the car increased while my odds of selecting the goat decreased. Therefore, selecting a 'new' door (switching) would be advantageous. I wonder if there is a threshold at which this methodology would break down or gain strength. What if there were n doors and Monty revealed n-1 goats? n-2? n-n/2?

I thought of a way to explain it to myself that I'll post in case it helps someone else. Suppose person A is transmitting letters of the alphabet to person B from far away. The equipment at each end is effected by extreme temperatures and/or extreme humidity causing voltage drifts. Therefore sometimes the letter received by B differs from the true one transmitted by A. Similar to morse code only in my example errors are normally distributed around the true letter. In other words, if the true letter is \O\", the most likely errors states are \"N\" and \"P\". If B constructs 95% confidence intervals showing that the letter is between \"M\" and \"Q\", he CAN say that based on past experience he is 95% percent certain that he put his intervals on either side of the the true letter. He CAN NOT say that the unknown letter has a 95% chance of being between \"M\" and \"Q\". If the true letter, unknown to B, is \"O\", \"O\" is either between \"M\" and \"Q\" or it isn't. \"O\" does not change its position in the alphabet randomly."

However, the issue of more variables than observations seems to be a valid big data issue that really cannot be avoided.It seems to me big data means we no longer have control over the sampling plan, or the space over which our data are collected, or the method by which it is collected. The genome we have is what we have. This in turn causes statisticians to look beyond traditional controlled environments to deal with the new level of, what I will term as, uncontrolled data. Meaning we did not control external sources of variation, nor did we control correlation between variables, nor did we control the range over which the variables will vary. Most scientists, when they write papers, are writing for their peers, in the peer review environment, for specialist publications like Elsevier or for presentation in conference.This introduces a couple of elements: - Peer review requires presentation of a certain professional *image* and the language that endorses that - They are writing for a specialist audience that are quite familiar with the vernacular. i.e., they are quite correctly addressing the audience level.Where the communication level has to change is when that material has to be tailored in order to make the information accessible to another audience type.For example, that original scientific paper may need to be taken and re-written so that it is understood by people that: - are going to provide funding for further research in that field - in writing training/educational materials based on the findings in the paper, for tertiary level students - for announcement through mass mediajust to provide a few examples, all of which require different re-writes to cater to totally different audience levels.

I have a very interesting format that I use for my students interactive math journals. I give them the notes - starting with their goal at the top. Then, the next session \what do you know about the goal\" requires student to give a few lines or ideas about what they already know. Then we share them as a class. The next session is \"What you need to know\" where I give them important vocabulary or previously learned concepts that connect to what we are learning. Then we go over examples. While reading about Ridge Regression, I got to wondering why it's called Ridge Regression. I was not satisfied by my web search. So, I would appreciate if someone would like to share. What I did learn is the method is also known as Tikhonov regularization. It is used to address the problem of multicollinearity, such as may be the case when time and world population may be two predictors in a model of global warming. Where time and population increase at a similar rate. Multicollinearity is quantified by the variance inflation factor or VIF, which we can find in the output of statistical software programs. When this is the case and we wish to keep both predictors in the model for valid reasons, we can use Ridge Regression to address the issues caused by multicollinearity. Such issues include changing coefficient estimates and p-values when a collinear predictor is added to the model. This makes interpretation very tricky. These issues are caused by the large variance of our coefficient estimates due to the determinant of our XtransposeX matrix being close to zero. Ridge Regression adds on a factor which penalizes large parameter values. This is the same as adding a factor to our XtransposeX matrix and results in a determinant far from zero. This in turn reduces the variance of our parameter estimates at the cost of now they are no longer unbiased estimates of the true value. Perhaps the determinant is the ridge?Ref: http://tamino.wordpress.com/2011/02/12/ridge-regression/

Is there a market for bitcoins where they can be easily sold and purchased?BitCoin is a digital currency that is based upon a special algorithm to create more currency and can be traded anyway where there is an internet connection. To convert from a hard currency to BitCoin or vice-versa...an exchange is needed which are located in most countries around the world. In essence, the exchanges act like a digital wallet facilitating BitCoin transactions between payor to payee. There are no geographical or time impediments where transactions are settled immediately once triggered.Does the decision to purchase bitcoins depend on their expected price increase or on some intrinsic value?The decision to purchase BitCoin would depend upon from what perspective you are viewing. If as an investor wanting a capital gain, it would be seen as a high risk option and would demand a high price for this being an unregulated area. There are also a number of other regulatory risks of transactions via BitCoin being banned which could result in a huge depreciation if this happened.If however, you were looking for a way to reduce the costs of a transaction without needing to go through a central bank where money was converted into BitCoin for a short period of time, then this is a different situation where the risk of loss is much greater.If you were to model the Chinese market for bitcoin, how would Chinese regulators' actions affect the risk and return? The more the Chinese regulators make it more difficult to transact via BitCoin, the more risky BitCoin will become to use. This will mean less people will want to use BitCoin and will demand a higher price as a result.Would a rational risk-averse investor add bitcoin to his/her portfolio? Would you?It is very unlikely that a risk-averse investor will ever invest in BitCoin being just too risk, especially as it is unregulated. Personally, I would not see it as an investment either...though...if seen from the perspective of international trade and facilitating international transactions where money is held in BitCoin for a short period of time...as in a Digital Wallet...then this is something I could consider. Though I would only hold a small balance of BitCoin rather than hold large sums of capital.

The circles are the 6 data points and the blue line isthe logit model predicted probabality:$p = {e^{(hateta_0 + hateta_1 x)}over{1+e^{(hateta_0 + hateta_1 x)}}}$![not perfectly separated - logit works OK][1]----Now here's a case where there *is* perfect separation.The glm fit wasn't happy about this, and gave thefollowing warning message: Warning message: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurredAnyway, here's where it ended up with:![perfectly separated - logit struggles][2]----In fitting, the *steepness* of the predictedprobability can be adjusted:![logit model curves][3]So in the second case, anything with an *infinite*steepness and a transition somewhere between $x=3$ and$x=4$ would fit the data *perfectly*. This means thethe fit isn't well defined.The infinite slope would mean that the left 3 points$mathrm{Prob}(y=1)=0$while the right 3 points have$mathrm{Prob}(y=1)=1$With these probabilities, the observed sample datawould be *certain*, so that would be the maximumlikelihood.But the infinite slope parameter and lack of uniquesolution create problems in the actual fitting.---- **Note about LDA**In the second case, with perfect separation, LDA should be fine.It will see two groups with equal priorprobabilities for each group. (3 points in each)It will see the groups centred about different means:- $y=0$ group with mean 2- $y=1$ group with mean 5and assume they both have the same standard deviation(of 1).So that if $x>3.5$ the probability will be higher forthe $y=1$ group and if $x<3.5$ the probability will behigher for the $y=0$ group. I think that the thinking about Gifted Education has shifted dramatically over the past ten years, and has been shifting for close to 15 years. I live in Fairfax Co., VA just outside of Washington, DC, and it has been considered one of the most progressive areas in the country for gifted education. [Not bragging so much as providing context] While we still have a system that 'groups' gifted kids for services at the elementary level (called Level IV Centers), the thinking amongst the Gifted leadership is that while Level IV Centers are a valuable opportunity for students to work with like-minded peers in a setting with high expectations and extended curriculum, it may not be necessary to achieve the desired outcomes through those centers exclusively. The county is in the process of expanding the capacity of all teachers to differentiate in any classroom, providing resource teachers to facilitate differentiation and learning, and raising the expectations for all students. We are working with a population where a significant number of students come to school well-prepared for a challenging curriculum, and whose parents are largely well-educated and committed to high standards for their children. [Again, not bragging]. I also know that many of the Title 1 schools lag behind on achievement benchmarks not because of staff limitations, but because the cultural/socio-economic factors affecting student achievement haven't been sufficiently addressed to ensure success for those students. I'm always interested in hearing from teachers/parents in less-affluent or metropolitan areas how gifted education is perceived and approached. I think it is dangerous to think that students can only be served in one particular model. I believe we need to move more toward Differentiation for Learning than separating populations based on perceived need at one particular time.

That's an interesting notion! In such a situation, you potentially **could** use Bayes' theorem. Here's a simple example:Let's say we know that one of A, B, C, and D have a disease. The probability for each is equal to 0.25. If we test the pool of A & B's blood, and the test is negative for the disease, you can apply Bayes' theorem and update the probabilities of A & B having the disease to 0. Similarly, if we test C & D's blood, and the test is positive, the probabilities of each of C & D having the disease becomes 0.5. You can then perform a test on C. If it's positive, C has the disease. If not, D has the disease. You have essentially reached the result with 3 disease tests, whereas the naive approach of testing each person's blood separately would take 4 tests.Raj

I have taught mixed level math classes for several years, but found that there were always several at the low end who found it difficult to keep up with the rest of the class and several at the top end who \got\" the concepts faster than everyone else. The faster students were not as much of an issue as the lower level students. Last year, we identified and grouped students who had learning issues into one class. On the first day of class, they looked around and knew why they were in that particular class, but were mostly happy not to be in the \"regular\" sections. We also added a support teacher and modified the lessons slightly, although the students still wrote all the same assessments as the other students. At the end of the year, many of these students were as or more successful than they had been in previous math courses. Even though they were separated into a different class, they felt that they were given extra support so they were willing to work hard to achieve success. In this case, grouping these students was the \"right\" thing to do. It also helped even out the other sections so that teachers were able to address more issues with specific students as they were more homogeneous in their abilities. I think that it worked for us because we had very specific documentation and previous assessments on our students so were very careful in choosing who were best suited for the grouped class. I know that it wouldn't work for every school, but it did work for us."

Hi there, the description you have provided is actually bagging. Random forest does an addition step in each iteration to select randomly from the p predictors, m < p predictors from which we use to decide the split. As for trees that does a really lousy job at classifying the test set, i believe a model does only as well as the data it is trained on. Therefore, it falls back to the question of whether the bootstrap procedure generates samples that are representation of the population. This in return is related to whether the main sample you are bootstrapping from has been collected well. Thus, if all is well, there shouldn't a reason not be democratic about it. After all, the deviations should even out in expectation/average according to large sample theories. (Note that we do monitor the error rate and estimate as the number of trees used increases)Hope this helps ~

Hello,nCr is a combination, what you are calculating is r objects taken from a set of n objects (without replacement and ignoring order). the formula for nCr includes n!, which is n \Factorial.\"For the lottery example, we are interested in choosing six numbers from 49 numbers. With 49C6 you are determining how many different combinations of 6 numbers can be formed from the 49 integers. Have you watched the optional module 2 video? I think this may be helpful! Half way through the video it discusses where the nCr formula comes from. Say you wanted to know how many 5 card hands you can draw from a deck, when order does not matter? If you do 52*51*50*49*48, this would be a permutation without replacement. We do not care about the order when it comes to 5 card hands you can draw from a deck. So we have to divide out order. How many different ways can you arrange five cards in five spots? ...5! ways (which is 120 ways). Now we have 52*52*51*50*49*48 / 5! this is equal to 52!/ ((52-5)!5!) which is the formula for combinations. The purpose of rent control, as I understand it, are to assure that those with the lowest level of income have rental housing available to them at a price they can afford when there is a shortage in the market. When there is a shortage of housing, people selecting the least expensive options will pay whatever they are able to, as the alternative is to be homeless (low demand elasticity). If the goal is to reduce homelessness, and/or to allow those in the lowest level of income to have some of the financial opportunities afforded to those at a higher income level (savings, insurance, pensions), then rent control seems a sensible approach. What the article seems to suggest is that in a shortage market with rent control, suppliers (landlords) will, rather than reducing supply, spend money on lawyers and lobbyists in an effort to reach achieve an equilibrium. If unfettered freedom of markets is the ultimate goal, then it is achieved, but if the goal has more to do with freedom of opportunity, the notion that a house is a human right, or the notion that when the lowest on the financial totem thrive, it benefits all, then I would conclude that legislators have failed.

Hi there, changing the order of columns will not affect the predictive power of the model. Theoretically speaking, orthogonalization is done via the gram schmidt procedure. That is, we use the first column as a reference and project the second column onto it to create an orthogonal vector. Then we use the third to project on the space span by the first and the second, and so on. Therefore, if you change the column of the design matrix it will certainly change the output of your orthogonalization. However, the seemingly different output is merely a rotation of the output you would have gotten if you have used the original orientation. Don't take my word for it. It is easy to see using say PCA for example. Note that if you change the columns of your design matrix, the loadings associated will change. However, the proportion of variation explained will remain the same.Hope this helps~

I think this situation is much more intricate than just these two options. First and foremost, I would expect that people who make minimum wage or near minimum wage may be enticed to not actively seek work if they are collecting federal jobless benefits; however, for people in higher income echelons they need to find a job with salary and benefits comparable to their old position so federal jobless benefits aren't very attractive to them. I think there is definitely some discrimination against those who have been unemployed for a long time, but at the same time, there are multiple factors going into this issue. You have to think that a lot of the people who became unemployed lost their job because the prospects within that industry had a very poor outlook. With this in mind, it is obvious that it would be very hard for them to find a new job if the entire labor market for say automotive marketing is doing extremely poorly post 2008. These people who have been unemployed for long periods of time could have been employed in an industry where the future prospects aren't looking very good so people are reluctant to hire. Furthermore, you have to consider the age of the person being laid off. For example, someone who is towards the end of their career but doesn't have enough saved to retire may have a very difficult time finding a job because any jobs that are within the sector are going to want younger candidates as the market recovers since they will be cheaper and with the company longer. If someone has been unemployed for a long time, you also have to think to yourself, \Why have they been unemployed so long?\" Many employers might discriminate against this candidate and think more lowly of their experience or skill set since other people within the industry haven't valued them enough to hire them."

Romain, you're working through the course very quickly! Yes, beta coefficients can be a huge source of confusion when authors/readers don't pay close enough attention to the units. Beta coefficients are entirely unit-dependent, so you have to think very carefully about the units. However, they do have some advantages over correlation coefficients. We often want to know how much does Y increase or decrease on average for a 1-unit increase in X. The correlation coefficient doesn't tell us this. A good alternative, for continuous variables, is to standardize them by turning them into Z-scores (Standard Deviation units). If you put standardized variables into the linear regression model, then the beta coefficients will be in SD-Y/SD-X units. So for example, a beta coefficient of 0.25 tells that a 1-SD increase in X correlates to a 0.25-SD increase in Y. It's easier to compare across different predictors if they are all in SD units.

One way to do this by hand is to organize it in a table and subtract the mean from each x-value. Hey guys,I,ll go one by one,In the first case:“The price of bicycles rose, and therefore the demand for bicycles went down.”This is incorrect because when it says \demand for bikes went down\" that would mean that the demand line shifted, which is not the case the demand just moved ON the demand line.Second:“The demand for bicycles increased, and therefore the price went up.”As the demand for bikes goes up (remember the demand line shifts, the demand does not move ON the demand line), the equilibrium price goes up. Third:“The price of bicycles fell, decreasing the supply of bicycles.”Same as in the first case, the supply of bikes does not change (for it to change there would have to be a shift in the supply line) the supplier is just willing to provide less at that price (movement ON the supply line).Last:“The supply of bicycles increased, and therefore the price of bicycles fell.”Same as the second case, as the supply line shifts down (up), it moves the equilibrium price down.Hope this helps,Regards,David."

Students form their identity around the courses they take. They have a math identity. By tracking or putting them in groups by ability, we are preassigning that identity for them and for everyone else. Student's assigned to certain \level\" of classes(low, medium, high, honors,etc) now have this image of themselves as that descriptor. Once student's are put in those classes and are tracked (which happens at an early age), parents, teachers and society buy in to said descriptor. Oh Tommy, he is ok at math but not great because he is the middle class. Or Jenny is great at math she is in honors. There is a lot of pressure and identity assigned along the continuum that supports a fixed mindset. Jenny is less likely to take risks in math lest she loose her standing. Upward or downward mobility is limited and those on the bottom will likely remain there through high school and those on top fear of losing this position. Mathematical achievement of the individual begins to ossify with this identity or class level. Students on the \"lower\" end will achieve to the standard that has been assigned and no more. While students at the \"higher\" end will also be cautious in achievement because they have been held to a higher standard of \"smart\" and do not want to loose it."

In general, those that live in rural areas, rather than urban areas, tend to be of a lower socioeconomic status and rely upon farming or subsistence agriculture or other forms of manual labour to survive and provide for their families. This makes people, especially women, more economically vulnerable, since we know that women have more difficulty finding jobs in the work force, and this problem is only further exacerbated in rural settings. The sexual division of labour often places women at home, where they have to tend to the children, prepare meals and clean the house. Often times the women have to fetch the groceries for meals, which could involve long and arduous journeys while tending to their children. Because rural families are more economically vulnerable in certain countries, they may not have enough food to feed their whole families. Men and children tend to eat more than the women, even if the mother is pregnant, nursing or ill. More often than not, the woman or wife is economically dependent upon her husband and may have been taken far away from her family after marriage. If she is domestically abused or assaulted, she may not have the financial means or social/family support system to escape her violent home, and thus may have no choice but to stay in a dangerous and painful situation. Some women think that their only option to escape grinding poverty and abuse is through suicide, even though many cultures condemn suicide and the families may be subsequently shamed. I'm speaking very broadly here and making a lot of generalizations, but I hope that gives a perspective on how multiple aspects of human life may conflate into the decision to end one's life for women in rural areas.

Imagine for example a data set with one data point per day. If you were to do normal bootstrapping with this, one bootstrap sample might have the data point for 4 May followed by the data point for 25 December followed by the data point for 15 March, and so on. In time series data, subsequent data points are often not entirely independent, so normal bootstrapping would mean losing these dependencies in the noise.To get around that problem at least a little, block bootstrapping groups data points into blocks (of equal size, ideally), and these blocks are then randomly recombined into bootstrap samples. For example, with a data set of one point per day, the block size could be 7, i.e. one week. A bootstrap sample might then have the data points for 27 February to 5 May, followed by the data points for 23 to 30 December, followed by the data points for 11 to 17 March, etc. Some dependencies are still lost (the data of 11 March follows that of 30 December, though it can't possibly depend on that), but the dependecies within blocks (of 12 March on 11 March, of 13 March on 11 and 12 March, etc.) are retained.

Grit can be trained. Elementary school students had to make plans for their play time, including contingency plans, etc. By doing this, they were able to sustain their play for much longer than without planning. Likewise, have you ever seen a focused seven-year-old at church, and an adult visitor who'd rather be texting than get through an hour of listening? The seven-year-old wasn't born focused, they were trained week in and week out to sit quietly, pay attention, be able to answer questions afterward, etc, and that's what makes the difference. Or have you ever seen kids, especially on farms, who do hours upon hours of chores, while other children would give up after ten minutes? Or marathon runners who choose to run 50 miles a week, while we choose to run 2 and stop, or never start at all... There certainly might be some innate portion of grit, but it can also be trained through routine habits.

Years ago when I was just beginning to experiment with student centered instruction I gave a problem like this to students in groups, and discovered that even those who could solve it didn't understand it. So I began with a simpler question: how much of the house would they paint in an hour working together? Of course that was a struggle for many 9th graders, but if they can't do that, there's no point in even asking the question given here. Similarly, a good mixture problem to start with is simply: if you mix 2 liters of a 20% acid solution with 3 liters of a 10% acid solution, what will be the acid concentration of the resulting liquid? It's shocking how hard that is, which shows that the more traditional version \how many of each should them mix to get. . .\" is pointless!"

The video \From Fear to Freedom\" was very well done and highlighted the problem of violence against women all over the world in many ways. The speakers in the film are fantastic and had such good thoughts and ideas. The problem is so overwhelming and widespread that it can invoke a sense of hopelessness. However, the ideas for methods to try to enact change leave one feeling a sense, that yes, this can be overcome. I particularly liked the idea of public education being used in conservative religious areas to educate about religious 'beliefs' that are actually not part of the Koran, Bible etc. I was a bit taken aback when Asmar Khader of Jordan said progress had been made in the area of 'honor' killings and they showed that some man had gotten a 3 month sentence! How appalling, but I guess it is better to start somewhere. Jacqeline Pitanguy of Brazil talked about police education, which I think is very important as often police are some of the first to be at the scene and have a reputation for looking the other way or taking a man at his word against the woman. Social media is a great idea in terms of reaching economically advantaged women but those who are more disadvantaged wouldn't really benefit from this. This also brings me to the concept of having more women involved in armies as soldiers or working in the policeforce, as was mentioned in the video, to try to 'level the playing field' so to speak. I think this does not work well. Think of all the cases of sexual abuse against female soldiers that are reported. I am certain that many many more are not reported. I loved what Mary Robinson said about the low rates of females being involved in peace processes. And the excuse is that women aren't trained? Give me a break! Just another way that men retain the power and control. She is correct, women need to be equal participants in these processes. I also appreciated the example of men being educated in Nigeria, in particular young boys, who can grow up without these preconceived ideas that they must control and abuse women and use violence against them. It is important to look at the big picture, and hard to be patient when this violence should stop NOW, but little by little, change will happen."

I think these are probably both correct. [Some people][1] say risk. [Others][2] rate. It just depends on the units. The inverse of the absolute difference in **rate** is 1/(events/person-time). That gives you person-time over events. If the rate difference is 0.01 heart attack/person-years, you need to treat 100 people for one year to see one more heart attack.The inverse of the absolute difference in **risk** is 1/(events/people). That gives you people/event.To illustrate, if the risk difference is 0.01 heart attacks/people, you need to treat 100 people to see 1 more heart attack. I think it's probably better to use the rate than risk if you have it so you can be more precise about what you're saying. Also, if you are asked this question by the professor, probably you should use what she said rather than what someone else wrote. Because additional trees are fitted to the *residuals* of the current fit. When the first tree is grown it is trying to fit the response as the response values are copied to the residuals vector. Then, a simple tree is fitted and a shrunken version of the fitted values is taken as the contribution of that tree to the fit. The residuals are reduced by the same amount, ready for the next tree.When the second tree is grown, because each tree is fitted to the *residuals* (**not** the observed values of the response), the fit is improved some more and with it the bias is reduced a little bit; the two trees more closely follow the data than the first tree alone.As you keep adding trees, those trees explain aspects of the data not well fitted by the set of previously fitted trees. If you were to keep on fitting, eventually you'd explain all the variance in the data: the residuals would be reduced to zero, and hence there'd be *no* bias [but high variance at that point]. Achieving this takes a long time with boosted trees because of the shrinkage applied to the fitted values of each tree; hence boosted trees learn slowly.Contrast this with randomForests; there we have the same ensemble of trees, but each tree is fitted to the same response vector of data/observations. The stochasticity introduced via bagging and the random selection of `mtry` parameters for each split-selection reduces models that can be averaged over, to reduce variance.I can't quite think of a good way to explain what is happening to the bias in a randomForest; you are fitting a (potentially) low bias, high variance tree to a bootstrap sample of the data because you grow each tree large, but as you only consider random subsets of the available variables to form each split in those trees, they could still be biased. How the two sources of bias contribute to the actual bias of the tree and hence to the ensemble of trees I'm not sure about. Perhaps someone will comment?

Labor is cost for a firm. Thus, the overarching goal for companies like Apple and Google is to keep labor costs low, as an extension of maximizing their profits. As with any kind of competitive market, competition between firms or entities is going to have the effect of driving the price for labor toward an equilibrium. The more demand there is for the labor - the more firms there are competing of the labor - the closer this price will be to the real, actual competitive market value.Thus, in the technology labor market, firms like Apple and Google can keep demand for labor artificially low by an agreement to not poach each others' employees. This type of collusion effectively amounts to an oligopolistic agreement between powerful firms to set market price from the other side - not by controlling supply but by controlling demand.

I teach at a high school with four-year grouping in math, and have never seen so many students (along with their parents!) with a fixed idea of what they \should be\" achieving and that they are or aren't \"math\" people, or are or aren't \"geometry\" or \"algebra\" people. The grouping does streamline our classes (I teach mainly honors and AP classes), but I've wondered for some time at what cost.We've had some interesting experiences working \"around\" grouping. These are a very small subset of experiences from a very specific arm of the possible approaches to math teaching, but I hope you find them enlightening.1) We have parents that fight their child's initial placement, usually because they think it's too low. We've ended up with a solution involving a sort of \"limbo\" Algebra/Geometry integrated course that delays the grouping decision for a year (but is taught at an accelerated level). While I feel we approach this in a more political way than I would like, I can see that it could have advantages; maybe we're reducing some of the \"borderline casualties\" doing it this way.2) For my part, I've tried to open up my AP Statistics class to more students; if a student REALLY wants to take the course, I let them -- our school has them sign a contract outlining the expectations for each AP course they take, so they know what they're getting into. (Disclaimer: I do use teacher recommendations for this process, and for each student, I discuss their past math and English achievement with them, as well as the AP Potential tool -- I try not to set students up for failure.) Our AP Calculus teacher also tries to find students in the \"middle\" groups who might be appropriately challenged by AP Calculus.3) Finally, it bears mentioning that all our math teachers look for students to move up and down from track to track from semester to semester. We usually don't move a student up until the year break, but we may move struggling students down when necessary. It's hard to say yet how these changes are working out for students long-term, as they've all been fairly recent. I know, though, that the AP math students in our school are developing persistence with a lot of support, and our test scores aren't too shabby -- most of our \"middle group\" students pass their AP exams, many with 4's and 5's. In one sense, I do think it gives credit to what we're trying to do with access and equity. In another sense, who knows what these students could have achieved if they had"

First may I say, I really am beginning to appreciate how forward thinking my school is!We don't stream (possibly an Aussie term but synonymous with tracking or setting) in our school ever, in fact, streaming is a dirty word in the Mathematics department. Instead we work in heterogeneous groups, including in my class at least, one student with learning disabilities that have him working two to four years below the others. We set the students to task and formatively assess those with a particular common error. We then ask these students to join what we call a fluid group, to focus on this particular area. Students know that, although they are part of the focus group one lesson that is not likely to be the case the next lesson. The key is their ability to move between groups.One thing I find though, is that students I now identify as being fixed mindset high achievers, sometimes seem to resent bein given the same materials to work on as the other students. We try to stretch these students horizontally rather than vertically. Ie. Rather than 'teach them the formulas to remember from the next year's work, which incidentally would not challenge them,we stretch their understanding of the concept by having them explore the overriding concepts and how they can be applied in new and novel ways. They despise having to explain their understanding and articulate their reasoning, but that is what they are required to do.

As a human, I am bound to make mistakes on a daily basis. Many of these occur while I am lecturing during my math classes. When this happens, I stay calm and try not to show embarrassment or frustration, even though all my students' eyes are on me watching my next move. I even joke a little and say \good thing I am not being graded on this\". A colleague of mine used to give extra credit points to the entire class if they caught her making 3 or more mistakes during a lecture. I usually step back and ask my students to help me find my mistake on the board, and in some instances the discussion that results from this finding process is very interesting and it helps reinforce the concepts being covered in class (like the validity of the method being used, or why is it that the answer in the back of the textbook looks different than mine, most common errors, etc.) I hope that my students learn from me that nobody is perfect (even teachers make mistakes all the time) and of course it is OK to make mistakes as long as you can learn from them."

Reliance on the biomedical model undermines the introduction of social interventions by fostering the assumption that non-medical interventions are inherently inferior and less effective than medical solutions. Non-medical interventions to epidemics like HIV/AIDS, however, can not only be effective, but have the potential, in a way that medical interventions do not, of creating long-term structural change that may prevent the spread of HIV/AIDS as well as numerous other diseases. Beyond disease prevention, the lasting implications of social interventions for human rights and quality of life are far reaching. For example, outreach and awareness campaigns aimed at eliminating myths and reducing the stigma of HIV/AIDS, and promoting (and providing) free, confidential testing, could reduce the incidence and fatality of the disease by making more people aware of their status and how to either stay negative or live healthily with positive diagnoses. Beyond this, it could lead to better treatment of people living with HIV/AIDS and fewer negative consequences associated with a positive diagnosis. If receiving a positive diagnosis had fewer social consequences, people would be much more likely to be willing to receive testing and learn about how to take care of their health, whether positive or negative.It is clear from the readings, however, that education alone is insufficient to prevent the spread of HIV/AIDS. Many women, for example, know the facts about the disease and how it is spread, and may even know they are at high risk of contracting HIV, but do not have the bargaining power to negotiate safe sexual encounters, or the social power to explore options for alternative ways of defining womanhood. Marital norms that give men sexual license while prohibiting women from advocating for their own needs, and expectations that women must bear children, make it difficult for women and girls the world over to negotiate for safe sex within a marriage, which can place her, and any potential children, at high risk for contracting HIV/AIDS. This suggests that structural interventions aimed at normalizing discussions of sex, sexuality and condom use within relationships - for both men and women - could help empower women to have a voice in their relationships, and empower both men and women to engage in healthier ways of relating. This could happen through comprehensive sex education, radio and TV shows that normalize the subject matter and make it accessible,

Are sit-down math problems during the summer the best way to help him overcome his math phobia? When I was seven, and my sister five, we had a computer game : Math Rabbit. We wanted to win all the prizes in the virtual carnival, so we would wake up on Saturday mornings, get on the MS Dos computer, and play for hours. Hardcore gaming wasn't a term back then, but that's what we would do with that silly carnival math game. It wasn't mom telling us to turn up the difficulty to Level 3 or Level 4, it was just us playing at a level we felt was easy enough to be fun, at the games we felt were fun.And that fun is way more useful than being able to borrow correctly at age 8. There's always age 9 for borrowing correctly, but he won't learn it then either if he's still afraid of math. Rereading this, it reminds me of an analogy to books - yes, your 8 year old CAN read challenging 3rd grade texts, but isn't it preferable if he's reading the easy Magic Treehouse series for hours and hours rather than crying at reading a page of a thick chapter book? Reading the easy books for hours will cause him to spurt in reading level so that before you know it, he's bringing home the Hobbit and Harry Potter book 7 to read because they look just as interesting as those \kiddy\" books he practiced reading on.]Seriously, find an online game that's below his grade level and let him play it to give him repeated opportunities of doing math problems correctly and feeling that the problems (and thus math in general) is \"easy.\" I have seventh graders on IXL who sometimes sneak and do all the 1st grade problems just because it is a confidence boost to score a 100%, even if it's for the skill Adding 1 - 10. Khan Academy is free and has these leveled-type problems too (though that site can be a pain to navigate).Alternatively, sit down with a deck of cards (1 - 10 only, Aces being 1) and play Addition War with him, so that he sees that math with mom can sometimes take the form of a game. Every boy loves a game called \"War.\" Make sure there's a \"I De-Clare War\" bout so that he has the fun of winning 10 cards at once. After a round or two, he'll decide its too easy, at which point you can graduate to Multiplication War. Again, you can use regular playing cards, or I have flashcards I got from Amazon that have fish on them. Here's my take:From Eq. (3.35) on page 90 of the book we haveif no student, slope is $eta_1$
if student, slope is $eta_1 + eta_3$Now look at the plot on the same page, the right pane. Clearly, the slope of the student line is less than the slope of the non-student line. You can see that the non-student line goes up, while the student line is kind of tilted towards the right side, wanting to touch the non-student line. So, we can say$eta_1 + eta_3 < eta_1$
$1 + eta_3 < 1$
$eta_3 < 1-1$
$eta_3 < 0$or, in other words, $eta_3$ must is negative. I hope it helps!

Though inequality sounds inherently undemocratic (just as Rousseau argued in the Discourse on Inequality that inequality is a form of enslavement), the article shows that inequality itself is not the ultimate problem to be fixed. It can both serve as the incentive for low income people to work harder and increase social mobility (and hence to realize the \American Dream\"). On the other hand, it also prevents many economic and social resources from flowing to the ones who most urgently need them, such as poor people and minority. This seem to me very paradoxical. It can be argued that the more inequality there is, the more incentive people have to work hard and change their fate. Also, more inequality means that it is harder for those unprivileged to succeed, which reduces their incentive to struggle to make a better life.Therefore, I think the effect of inequality has to be curbed, but not eliminated, just like nuclear power. For decades people have tried to eliminate inequality, and the entire Communist Manifesto focuses on creating the world for proletarians in which everyone is equal economically and socially. Many have tried, and some North European countries have actually become socialist welfare states, but radical revolution to abolish inequality certainly did not work well in China, Cuba or USSR, nor will it ever work to fix the current problems in the developing countries as well as in USA. The article said that \"too often high-tax welfare states turned out to be inefficient and unsustainable\", but lower tax also did not solve the problem. So I am mostly confused what to do and hope to learn more economics before I form my own opinion."

As government decided to enforce rent control in SF, it would be impossible to keep housing price low while keeping the market efficient. As landlords build pricey houses to cater to high-income people, their housing supply for people who have low- and moderate-income decreases. The housing quantity in the market will be less than the equilibrium quantity;therefore, a deadweight loss appears.In order to attain the goal of Pareto efficiency, there are some conditions that need to be satisfied, like MB=MC; however, when government began to enforce rent control, the marginal benefits of consumers could never be equal to the marginal cost of producers. The only way that could reach market efficiency is to create a competitive market environment and let the \invisible hand\" drive market to obtain efficiency.But taken the market trend in SF into considerations, people may find it's hard to not intervene the market when more landlords constructed pricey houses instead of providing fair-price housing for moderate- and low-income people."

anonymous, I don't know how this is done - but how would you do it, if you had to invent your own method?If I had to invent my own method, I might think of a few things:- the first thing I would try would be to use the whole data and compare between the winning models coming from cross-validation using AIC or BIC- the second thing I might try would be to do forward/backward step-wise selection of predictors, using only the set of predictors that had appeared at least in one of the winning modelsOther things I might try:- if I could, I might gather more data and do cross-validation on it using only the predictors that had been significant in at least one (or at least a fraction, or perhaps in all the models fitted for the first set of data)- if I couldn't gather more data, I might be tempted to throw away the predictors that are clearly non-significant, and redo the cross-validation with a fewer number of predictors- in fact, I would try to redo the cross-validation over a number of different random splits of the data, and hope the process converges onto a common modelWould such a 'hierarchical' cross-validation process be correct, statistically - what do people think?I am curious, too, what is the 'official' answer - how this is done in practice.

I don't work in a classroom - I tutor homeschooled and special needs students who need a different way of teaching for each one. I can imagine it is hard to try to accommodate everyone's needs but I do think it can be done. When my sons were in lower elementary school, their classes had stations or centers for both math and language arts. I helped in the classroom and saw how the center offered a chance for each student to work at their own level on same or similar tasks. In response to a prompt that often was built around the monthly theme of homes or gardens or the circus, etc., one student might have been using base 10 blocks to model a problem with adding tens and ones, while another used pencil and paper to add and carry 3-4 places, or another drew images showing addition, and so on. A parent helper or aide would be there to offer support and inspiration. Or sometimes it was set up so that one station had similar but more complicated tasks, the next had easier concepts or tasks, and the third, a simpler task yet, and the student could choose the task that was at their level. This worked well and instead of grades, the school used a rubric for evaluation. But I also recently watched my husband try the function task Jo gave us with the block towers and I thought he wouldn't be able to figure it out (I had found 4 different ways to look at the function) but he surprised me with a very different way of looking at the problem and I could see that his math esteem had grown when I told him that! I wish he had had a teacher who could have worked with him in his best way of learning or that he could have worked in a center-based math class with manipulatives. Anyway, I like the idea of the growth mindset classroom, as it allows many more children the opportunity to be part of the learning community with their classmates and offers collaboration and problem-solving practice for higher level thinking and tasks.

So the information provided allows you to create a contingency (2x2) table. We are given percentages, so it is best to just make up a baseline number of patients of 100 (in order to fill out the table).If you make the top row \fracture\" and the bottom row \"no fracture,\" then you can fill out the row totals of 30 and 70. For the columns, you can make the left column >55 age and the right column =<55 age. Here \"marker\" means an \"indicator\" or something that identifies. So we use this as our \"test +\" and \"test -\" columns. This is the + or - marker for fraction!You are then told that 60% of the patients with a fracture are older than 55... So in the \"fracture\" top row, 60% are >55. To solve for this, you take the row total = 30 and multiply it by .60 (for the 60%). This gives you 18. With a row total of 30 and the \"Fraction/>55\" intersection of 18, you can solve for the \"Fraction/=<55\" intersection.You are then told that 60% of the patients without a fraction are older than 55. You have the row total for \"no fraction\" = 70 ... so (.60*70) = 21. Just like above, you can solve for the \"No Fraction/=<55\" intersection. Now your table is filled out and you can calculate the column totals!Next you want to think about what the negative predictive valueNPV = # True Negatives / # testing Negatives"

If we are to counter these things of which we are not conscious in our interactions with others we must do so consciously and conscientiously. We have to make a point to compensate.For me I find this easier to do with young kids than with adults sometimes. When it is a parent about my age that I am working with I feel that I more easily slip. thoughts will pop into my head and I will think, \"where did that come from?\" Or with young adults I often do the same. Quick judgements can be very accurate, but we need to also question them reflectively and ensure that we are not perpetuating negative typing.We will never completely get rid of stereotypes. Our mind sorts automatically to create quick judgements of people. It is how we have evolved. The standard error of the sample mean can get from two methods1. From the mathematics, it's need the standard deviation of the population. Then plug in to the formula: s.err = population standard deviation/(n)^0.5. However it is meaningless because in this case, we already know the population standard deviation.2. From the computer simulation, we select the sample with size 100 for 10,000 times. So we can get the standard error of the sample mean. Then plug in to the formula: s.err = population standard deviation/(n)^0.5. Here you go. We can gain the population standard deviation. But it's meaningless because for the computer simulation we put the parameters of true mean and population standard deviation. We already know the population standard deviation.However based on the second methods, if we can select the sample with size 100 for 10,000 times from real experiment. For instance, we pick up 100 men to measure the Vitamin D level and repeat it for 10,000 time. Then we can get the standard error of the sample mean. Then plug it into the formula. At the end we can get the population standard deviation.

Sometimes to illustrate the point, I have two students come to the front of the room and I give them trays that they hold in front of themselves. I teach high school chemistry and I have two levels of groups: a regular level and honors level. My observations are of two sides: 1. I cannot imagine these two levels mixed! My high achievers would be bored to death and will just not show up. My lower level students will get lost, will shut out, and will not show up either. I believe that, at least in high school, division into ACHIEVEMENT levels (not ABILITY levels!) is very important. It provides an opportunity for better prepared students to stay interested and engaged, and learn more. It also affords students less prepared more time to work on the concepts and, again, learn more.2. On another hand, the division is far from perfect and each of these groups contains mixed levels. There are students placed in the wrong level, either higher or lower than fits their readiness level. In all cases it would be a disaster to ignore the obvious disconnect. I think that ANY two students are on two levels and require differentiated approach. It is up to the teacher to recognize \misplaced\" students and prepare materials on a few levels inside each grouping. It is very hard to do, no doubts. With our already super-busy overload, it is hard to find the time and energy to prepare multilevel lessons, however it is critical for student learning. In addition to that, we all stay after school to help those who still need more time and more training.So the bottom line: achievement grouping is needed (at least in high school math and sciences), but it does not replace a teacher who teaches all learners on many levels."

I have evaluated some essays of my peers. As a reader/reviewer of scientific papers, I understand and agree with the necessity to reduce the introductions (*e.g., 3 paragraphs*), but I have afraid that some new writers aren't taking the message perfectly. For examples, I read and evaluated some introductions that explained about research protocols using rats – animal experiments. They explained how and what previous experiments had performed, as well as what the experiments had not performed. In my opinion, **they were very good in relation to the determined criteria to evaluate the essays**, but they were **empty in context** – *e.g., what are the connections with humans?* For me, rats are only a method to study and to represent the mechanisms in humans, and we should become clear the connections for the reader/reviewer to justify a study. In short, in this course I’m learning a lot of good strategies to reduce the size of papers, but this **doesn’t mean to impoverish them**. We don’t need to perform a review about the theme in the introduction, but we should **keep the context**.Best wishes

In theory, I believe education should be a public good, but in our current school systems it is not. There is a massive quality gap in education that is a result of public versus private funding, quality of the school district, presence of charter schools, and institutional practices. Because the quality of education is not the same across the nation, it cannot be deemed a public good in the same way that national defense can. Education is rivalrous and excludable. It is excludable because certain social injustices prevent certain types of students from accessing education in the same ways that other students are able to. For example, individuals coming from various socio-economic statuses may have varying resources to buy school supplies, receive help on homework, receive proper nutrition for development, etc. As compared to national defense where anyone receives protection regardless of race, class, or gender, education does not provide the same blanket coverage. Additionally, education is rivalrous. I would consider education to be a limited resource, especially higher education such as college. There's no such thing as a \project\" in R. That's an RStudio term. When you create and save a <default> project, RStudio will save a workspace image that preserves the objects you had created in a directory with the project name. It also saves a history file of the commands you gave. When you use Open Project from the Project dropdown you can choose the .proj file that has the needed information to resume a session. \"Workspace images\" and \"history files\" are ordinary R concepts. Try `?save` and `?history` for their help pages. You are really learning two systems at the same time if you are using RStudio. Some systems (Windows and Macs) will hide the .Rdata and .history files from teh default file browsers. I run my Mac with \"invisible\" (or \"dot\") files made visible."

The group means are the same, andso their separation, as before, is greater in the x<zipRedac> directionthan in the x2 direction.But here, the variance in the x<zipRedac> direction is much largerthan the x2 direction. So despite the greater separation ofgroup *centres* in the x<zipRedac> direction, the greater variance inthat direction makes it appear that the separation is not as\"clean\" in that x<zipRedac> direction as in the x2 direction.The linear discriminant plot reflects this — it shows the\"distance\" (taking the variance into account) between groups<zipRedac> and 3 to be greater than between groups <zipRedac> and 2.R outpu"

Laura, your suggesting is like a binary search algorithm. Eg. in video example, if there's 100 people and only 1 person has the disease, then it would only take 7 tests to determine who had it. Test a pool of 50. If negative, look at the other 50; if positive then look at this 50. Test a pool of 25 remaining. If negative ... So 7 tests will get you the one person.The problem becomes if more than 1 person has the disease. One possibility is to shuffle the pools and test them. The number of pools and shuffles would have to be large enough that we could distinguish a single person from another person. Going back to your example, suppose we have 8 people (1-8). Suppose we first pool P1(1,2,3,4) and P2(5,6,7,8) and test both. Now we shuffle the pools so that we have P3(1,2,5,6) and P4(3,4,7,8) and test both. And shuffle again P5(1,5,3,7) and P6(2,4,6,8). Here are our possibilities:If 1 positive -> P1, P3, P5 positiveIf 2 positive -> P1, P3, P6 positiveIf 3 positive -> P1, P4, P5 positiveIf 4 positive -> P1, P4, P6 positiveIf 5 positive -> P2, P3, P5 positiveIf 6 positive -> P2, P3, P6 positiveIf 7 positive -> P2, P4, P5 positiveIf 8 positive -> P2, P4, P6 positiveSo if our test show P1, P2, P4, P6 to be positive, then we know persons 4 and 8 are positive. If P1, P3, P4, P5 show positive, then we know persons 1 and 3 are positive. If P1, P2, P3, P4, P5 positive, then persons 1, 3, 5 and 7 are positive. So in this case, we required 2xlog2(N) tests instead of the log2(N) tests required for the binary search case. So, effectively, we could determine all the positives in a population of 100 by conducting 2xlog2(100) ~ 14 tests regardless of the prevalence of the disease.I think my logic is correct. But I'd like to hear what others think about this strategy.

I found this year that warning them ahead of time that I would be asking them to discuss and share their thinking including their mistakes was really helpful. But I also followed that up by recognizing that it could be scary, uncomfortable, and frustrating, but that I would try my best to guide them and be understanding. I told them it was okay to get mad at me and it was okay to share frustrations. We started the very first week by brainstorming what they thought would be hard about the expectation and what scared them the most. Then we set about choosing sentence starters and questions that would help them sort through their fears and navigate discussions in a productive way. I also had to stop and take a lot of calming breaths and remind myself to ignore time and address the mistakes in the prescribed manner. I think my own brain grew a lot this year considerring the number of mistakes I made in responding to mistakes. However, the kids were really willing to let me take a do over and say that's not the way I want to respond to mistakes...let's go back and see what we can learn here. They were extremely forgiving when I fell prey to the time pressure. Next year I hope to be more transparent in my own learning curve with them, because I find that they will stop and remind me.
