The human rights approach is essential to women's health but it is a very difficult sell (so to speak) to policymakers and leaders in developing countries who are mainly interested in economic development. This is not an indictment on the character of these persons but rather comment on the assimilation of a human rights approach into local cultures. In many cases a woman's rights are viewed through a lens of economic development and the woman becomes a means to an end. Nations create work opportunities for women which are part time, unskilled, low paying, in hazardous environments or even create underemployment simply to meet international pressure to provide work. In the some cases of women's health may not be viewed a fulfillment of a right to access quality care but rather as a means of limiting associated to a social 'ills' or financial inconveniences. I think this is because the health issues of women are still viewed as the concerns of an 'other': concerns of persons outside the realm of concern and control of persons with power (more often male, older, non poor). Working in women's health requires advocacy which targets changes in cultural perceptions of women's issues. To a certain extent the domestic violence data showing women being more supportive of GBV is not surprising to me. In many (not all) situations where women are abused, there is an underlying culture of abuse as an acceptable form of social interaction and sanction which transcends women's issues. It is no coincidence that many of the societies which neglect women's issues also have problems stemming from cultural norms which have entrenched the either/or paradigm. The human rights approach must move beyond policy strategies and and conceptually isolated programmes to become cultural movements. The success of the groups identified depends on their and their allies capacity to positively affect local cultures.

I work in a small school in England and we have only 29 pupils in the whole school. I teach the key stage 2 class (children aged from 7 years old up to 11 years old), so that is four year groups in one class. I teach the same problem to the whole class, and make sure it is open enough for all children to achieve on their level. The children sit in family groups (mixed ability) so an 11 year old might be sitting next to a 7 year old and they often help and support one another. Sometimes I set the children different challenges and differentiate the work specifically but then I let them choose their own challenge. Most children choose appropriately. From an English forum (http://forum.wordreference.com/showthread.php?t=<phoneRedac>): You should consider how IN and ON are used –IN can mean within, inside, into, with, surrounded by, amongst, and, with transport, by,“In the box is a message.” Within“What is in the box?” inside“Put it in the box.” Into“I climbed in a taxi” into“In the trees there is a house.” Surrounded by\one in seven people said that it was bad.\" amongst“I went to the palace in a taxi.” By taxi (note how the indefinite article is omitted.)ON can mean –on top of, onto, at the time that is, and, with transport, by, in, inside.“The vase is on the table.” on“He put the picture on the wall.” Onto“On Thursday, I will go to town.” at the time that is“I will go to Beijing on a train.” By train (note how the indefinite article is omitted.)“On the train there is a restaurant.” In“Seven bandits are on the train.” In/insideSo both words have different meanings but their meanings overlap. You will see that, for clarity, it is often better to use the word that best expresses your intended meaning."

Teaching students the way they will be tested is a research-based principle. Kudos to you! Study guides with procedures? Not everything has to be a problem-based learning. Indeed, much research suggests that problem-based learning works better if a student has background knowledge. Notice, no where did I say that the problem-based learning needs to be exactly like the procedures already taught. By giving students a path, they can be prepared to think deeper and in a different way. Also, repeated attempts at the same procedures in novel concepts increases memorization and understanding. There should be a mix! So many want to act like worksheets are fake and problem-based learning is authentic. Learning comes in all different styles and methods; for example, Japan does a great deal of memorization before problem-based learning. Other countries, like Germany, do very little problem-based learning. Yet, both countries are at the top of the world's math tests. The point is that like solving a math problem, there are several paths to teaching.

I agree that students do far too little thinking on their own, and need to do tasks that encourage exploration and free thinking. But I have found that after a great round of activities establishing the relationship between the area of a rectangle (or parallelogram) and the area of a triangle, a month later my students need some practice with the formula. So we need quality tasks that help with recall and automaticity. And I think for fractions and some algebra work, bar models or tiles or other manipulatives/models are great, and there are times when the students need to be trained and exercised in using those models, so they have the competence required to use them before we present a challenging problem for exploration or sorting out."

I substitute teach and I want to share an experience I had in 3rd grade, several years ago. Even though groups in this class weren't formal, the students in the class definitely knew who was in low/high math groups. This is a school that uses no grades for k-5. I was working with a group of students on a project to determine supply amounts for a trip. This particular student came up with a response that no one in the group agreed with. I sensed it was because she had a reputation that she wasn't good in math (the teacher had told me she would need extra help for math). I don't remember the specifics of the problem, but I do remember that she was adding a column of 2 digit numbers. I verified (to myself) that her answer was correct and she could duplicate and explain her thinking. No one else in the group could correctly add the column of numbers. The other students in the group told me that she couldn't have the correct answer. We discussed different strategies and then someone suggested we use a calculator to see who had the correct answer. She beamed when the calculator agreed with her response. I was sad to see that the students had labeled her in their own mind as someone who couldn't do math. I saw that not only do fixed mindset strategies affect the individual student, they also affect peers to not respect a \less smart\" learner. From the data presented in the table, it is clear that the proportion of U.S. GDP devoted to consumption has increased over the previous five decades. Moreover, net exports went from a surplus to a deficit during this period, which also reflects Americans growing appetite for goods and services.Because Americans have been consuming more, they necessarily have been saving less of their incomes than before. With less being saved as a proportion of income, it is no wonder that the share of GDP devoted to investment has gone down as well. Thus Americans appear to be more focused on satisfying their wants and desires in the present and have become less concerned about focusing on the future.Canada, on the other hand, has not consumed as much as Americans as a proportion of GDP and in fact consumption to GDP declined slightly in the 50 years period shown in the data. This allowed Canadians to save more and these savings allowed for a greater proportion of GDP to be devoted to investment. The less rapaciousness of Canadians and their willingness to devote more income to investment is reflected in trade surpluses and a general increase in net exports over this time. So over the last 50 years or so, the American consumers were devoted to satisfying their own appetites while Canadians decided to consume less as a proportion of GDP so they could save and invest more in an effort to satisfy the appetites of Americans and foreign consumers as reflected in the increasing trade surpluses.One other thing may be mentioned. The proportion of GDP devoted to government expenditures declined in the U.S. while it went up in Canada, despite the huge expenditures that were spent for war and to save the financial system in the U.S. There is a sentiment of distrust in government that has held sway with many Americans over the years that has limited the growth of government expenditures that does not appear to be as strong in Canada.

While reading is necessary (although I remember attending a Masters seminar on Proust in which one of my classmates delighted in making contributions that superficially appeared to be based on his perusal of the material when in fact he rarely kept up with the assigned reading) most readers do not think of reading as an effort. But math requires actual effort. If at first you do not succeed, you must try again, rewrite the problem, learn to sleep on it, learn to push yourself to do what you do not find easy. Math is the vegetable portion of school offerings. It is good for you but you must 'develop a taste for it.'Naturally, many adults including parents and even educators persist in propagating a set of negative messages. Some parents complain that they have never 'used it' after completing school. One principal memorably informed me that our mutual student should not be concerned if she were unable to do the same math as her classmates since there are CEO's who cannot spell or do math. The implicit message was that our friend would be successful even though she would never 'get it'. A current problem is that in this province at least, several factors combine to make math harder than in the past. The curriculum was prepared with the notion that calculators must be involved since they exist. Add to that the low esteem in which memorization is held and you have an activity based exposure to math concepts, many math concepts and insufficient practice so that no mastery results. Middle school students start to struggle the moment operations on fractions require them to find common factors. No times tables means no number sense. Often their teachers bow to time pressures and provide them with copies of the times table instead of encouraging them to skip count or write the multiples out. The PP students learned how to select and sort information. They read carefully and decided what previous knowledge was applicable to the new situation. The math they had done in class was not compartmentalized and the teacher did not demonstrate how to do the problem first. They probably did problems that were multi-step, multi-concept and had little scaffolding. If you want to make small steps in a traditional classroom, here are a few suggestions:1. Ask the question first. It can be a question you already have, and it does not have to be a great open ended question. Do this right from the beginning of the year so it sets the \tone.\" You will get complaints at first that you are not \"teaching\" but the students adjust. Find a problem that has more than one way to do it. Don't show them what to do, just let them work and ask questions. Hopefully you will get more than one solution to discuss with the class. For example- Give the students 3 ordered pairs and ask if they are all on the same line. It can be done by graphing, comparing slopes, writing the equation a line through 2 points and showing the third point is on it, or using distances. If a student finds one method quickly, challenge him to find another. 2. Don't label or tell the students the \"topic\" of a problem before you do the problem. This takes away the necessity of thinking about what skill to use. AFTER you have finished the problem ask the students how they think the problem should be labeled. What was the key idea? What skills did they need?3. Take the scaffolding out of the problem and let the students figure out what they need to do. Let them decide on their own process. Sometimes you will be surprised; they find methods better than yours. Have the students work in groups and you can walk around to answer questions and give hints if necessary. When the class is done, the students can generalize the thought process or procedure for the solution.4. If you review, don't go in any order or have labels on the problems. Mix everything together so the students have to think and figure out what concepts apply.5. When I test, I always make sure there are a couple of problems that require concepts from previous units. If there is concept from an earlier unit that caused difficulty, revisit it during class and include it on the next test. If a student thinks he is never going to be asked about a topic again, he will not remediate. There needs to be incentive to corre"

I teach 6th grade--they are social beings, if I can add a social component, I've increased engagement and success potential1- more heads are better than one--imagine a world from only 1 person's perspective...very limited and dull. 2- more heads are better than one--increases pool of experience thereby increasing background knowledge that can be applied to make connections that couldn't be made alone or with less. Ideas will have multiple people making sense thereby decreasing the possibility of misdirections. 3- more heads are better than one--it is just more fun, we can all row together when energized, or some can pause their rowing, while others keep the boat going forward. In the end we've all made it to the shore without sinking the ship. 6 Eyed Monsters: The challenge is making sure the groupings support each other...a culture of collaboration needs to be nurtured. Math \wizzes\" need to appreciate multiple approaches/voices and \"slow\" processors need to be confident that they are a valued participant. Groups of 2, 3, 4, 5, 6...what works best? The bigger the group, the easier it is for students to check out. I like groups of 2 for practice work. Keeps both engaged and they can support each other. Groups of 3 if I want everyone on when problem solving, however I can rarely set this up due to various restraints (space, time, teacher connections, whole class connections).I have 30 students with 6 tables so I usually work with groups of 5. They each have individual whiteboards for individual thinking and group whiteboards for group thinking. The pens are color coded for individual input. On occasion the students use the iPods or iPads to film their problem solving process. They then review the video for what did and didn't work. The goof off level decreases when they are on video (after the novelty wears off), and become a great tool for reflection. I use the videos for formative assessment. We also share parts whole class with permission for participants to highlight important practices and strategies."

One idea that struck a chord with me was that the new standards (Common Core) require students to develop a persistence in solving math problems. This is going to be a huge challenge for students! Students are not currently being held to this standard. Students will need to develop math rigor, similar to how students have to develop an increasing rigor with reading - longer texts, harder texts. Students too often are being spoon-fed information and just filling in answers. Students will now be required to process large amounts of information and apply various procedures and operations and then interpret the results to derive an answer.Another idea that struck me was the quote by the columnist, \...invention and discoveryemanate from the ability to try seemingly wild possibilities. To feel comfortable being wrong before being right...\". I totally agree with these views. Our world has changed and is changing. Students are being tasked with the need to problem solve as never before. The most successful problem solvers are the ones who are willing to try outrageous solutions and are willing to take risks and make mistakes. As a teacher, I must reward thinking and risk-taking over correct answers. As learners, students must endeavor to be creative, take chances, and expect to be wrong."

They show convincingly the major role of they sympathetic system when we are subjected to stresses involving \negative\" emotions like fear, anxiety, uncertainty and anger.I would like to point out, however, that the sympathetic system may also be heavily involved when we are having \"positive\" emotions, especially those concerning pleasure. A prime example is during sex, when the heart rate and blood pressure rise to levels typical of those of a long distance runner racing for the tape. Blood pressure and heart rate also rise considerably during simpler pleasures. I find my systolic blood pressure rising as much as 20 or 30 mm whenever I read anything that I enjoy very much, Winnie the Pooh, for example. It also rises considerably whenever I eat anything that I enjoy, or laugh at a good joke. In addition, I think the psychologists will tell you that any type of focused attention involves a jolt of adrenaline.Because of Walter Cannon physiologists and doctors have focused on the \"fight or flight\" aspects of the sympathetic system. Why do we talk about the feeling of floating in a lecture on microgravity? Because floating (in space) ***is*** microgravity. If we ignore the sun and stop all motion, there is only one place in the earth-moon system where gravitational pull is zero -- at point near the moon where where the pull of the smaller moon is equal and opposite to the pull of the earth. But in practice, there are many other situations in space where we feel the effects of microgravity.This is because gravity and acceleration are indistinguishable from one another, so acceleration can counter the gravity of a body in space in just the same way gravity does. Free fall is accelerating at a rate that counterbalances gravity, and if you are free-falling in plane with no windows you will be floating within the plane.*Floating is free-fall, and free-fall is zero gravity.*The special thing about a stable orbit is simply that you can be in free fall indefinitely.

To further prove that education is not a public good, there are examples of private schools and even state colleges require tuition which excludes students that can not afford it. I love so much about math and this topic is one of the things I love. We as a society are so focused on teaching all students algebra that much of the beauty and power that is mathematics is lost. The subject is dry and uninteresting and boring to teach as we currently do in the K-12 system. My children have gone through public high school and the math I have seen there is the most boring stuff I have ever seen. In an effort to ensure all students have the computational skills down we have taken the fun out. In fact most teachers are so busy ensuring that they get through the computational tasks that there is no time to play or explore unique approaches to answering questions. I would love to see 7th and 8th grade math focus on questions like the golden ratio and computing amortizations and the history of unit measure rather than pushing Algebra to younger and younger grades.
